> @uiri Why does it matter if it breaks the test suite if the test suite is incorrect? 

If the test suite is incorrect then it should be fixed. I rely on the test suite when I make bug fixes and add in features related to new versions of the spec. The test suite in question is also language agnostic - improving the test suite will also improve other TOML implementations. This promotes interoperability when multiple different implementations (in different languages) use the same TOML files.

> Further, the test suite is outdated: TOML is a v0.4 while the suite tests for v0.2.

I do not think that the test suite is outdated. The version which it claims to support is not necessarily the latest version which it actually supports.

> If you think the changes are correct, then why does it matter if an incorrect test suite fails?

For a number of different reasons all stemming from the fact that maintenance work on this library is sporadic and my memory is, to put it mildly, less than perfect. I'm sure I've accidentally reintroduced bugs due to missing tests for them in the test suite before. I want to prevent that from happening again.

> In any case, `pytoml` does the right thing here, and I'm fairly certain (though I haven't tried) that these changes will pass a fork of @BurntSushi's test suite: https://github.com/avakar/toml-test/

The forks of the test suite maintained by @Avakar and @BurntSushi respectively have now diverged to the point that ideally I would have *both* of them passing until @BurntSushi merges @Avakar's tests in.

Just a few questions that arose in my mind:

- Is there any specific reason for S3PrefixedStorage? Couldn't it be just an option of S3Storage that can be provided or not?
- Yep, I think that keeping the prefix only on the storage and not leaking it to the file_id would lead to a more coherent behaviour. It would behave as all the other storage options. Otherwise we have a special case where changing that option actually doesn't involve past files (as the prefix is stored with them). Which might lead people to think that also all the other options behave like that or vice versa.

Disabling it would practically break anything in database integration, so it's not possible to disable it.
Also you are not required to change the depot_name, you can just change the prefix in the configuration if I understood what you are trying to do.

DEPOT itself doesn't make any assumption regarding the features of the underlying storage, so it doesn't provide a feature to move files without re-uploading them as that is something not all storages might support (E.G. Gridfs doesn't support moving documents between collections without reinserting them). If you are migrating files from a place to another using a Storage specific feature you are on your own also on updating all the DEPOT metadata.

> Aside of this, we are thinking in using this library to serve (very) private files instead of public ones. 

Just avoid using the `DepotMiddleware` and serve the files yourself.

If your concern is preventing people from trying to guess the ids, I think you can easily apply HMAC with a secret to the IDs before sending out the URLs that include those, as the URLs will be generated by the same backend that has to read them (or at least one that can share the same secret). At that point any attempt at trying with a different ID will be invalid and you can decode back the id from the HMAC before looking up the files.

> Maybe I could implement it on my codebase making a class that inherits from LocalFileStorage and overrides the create method, that is where uuid1 is called.

Going through the previous suggestion would make this unrequred as any id used by depot will be masqueraded by the hashing you apply to it, but If you want to submit a pull request to allow `LocalFileStorage `, `MemoryFileStorage `, `awss3.S3Storage` and `boto3.S3Storage` to accept a `id_generator` argument which by default is `uuid1` (to retain compatibilty) I'll gladly merge it, so you can use uuid4 or any other kind of unique id

In [further conversation](http://p.anticdent.org/3KVS) we agreed that stripping query string is bad.

So the summary is that $LAST_URL will be: the value of test_data['url'] used in the last request after and replacers ($RESPONSE, $LOCATION etc) have been processed but without query_parameters processing and without host expansion.

Unless @jd has further comments that change this idea, I'll implement it soon so we can get this out of the way and move on to content handlers.

I've been thinking about HTTP a bit.  All things considered, I think I'd prefer the "curio" project itself to be focused more on the core task execution model than to be an all-inclusive framework.  This would make it more like asyncio (as opposed to something more expansive like Twisted).  For example, asyncio doesn't include any kind of HTTP library--that's provided by other third-party modules such as aiohttp.   I see Curio in a similar model. 

On plugin-systems:  implementing things like that are often a tricky affair and can introduce considerable complexity to the whole module/package/packaging infrastructure of curio.  I'm not sure there's much to be gained by having everything live under the top level 'curio' namespace.  For example, there's no plugin system for asyncio, but I'm not sure if it's worse-off for not having it.  We could always reconsider later, but for now I'd probably stay away from doing that. 

Big picture:  I am not personally working on an HTTP implementation for curio nor do I have plans to write one (I'd much rather use one written by someone else).  However, if someone is writing one, I want to make sure that people can find it somehow.  So, there should be a documentation effort to link to such projects.  Maybe grouping such projects into a "python-curio" organization of some kind.  At a minimum, making sure such projects make it to the Curio README file perhaps.

You always get a uni-part index if `-I` is larger than the total lengths of all sequences. You can set it to something very large like `-I 10000G` (if this doesn't work, let me know). The total memory will be determined by the total length, not by the actual `-I` value.

If you have a prebuilt index, you can use the following command line to see how many parts it contains:
```sh
grep -obUaP "MMI\x2" index.mmi
```
Note that this only works with GNU grep. Mac uses BSD grep by default, which does not work well with binary files.

One example I have is from reaction kinetics. Suppose we have an equilibrium reaction between compound A and B with rate constants `k_forward` and `k_reverse`. Then these can be expressed in terms of activation energy through Arrhenius. But for the activation energy of the reaction, we might know that `Ea_reverse > Ea_forward`.

Therefore:
```python
Ea_r, Ea_f, A_r, A_f = parameters('Ea_r, Ea_f, A_r, A_f')
R, T = 8.3145, 298

k_f = A_f * exp(- Ea_f / R * T)
k_r = A_r * exp(- Ea_r / R * T)

model_dict = {
    D(A, t): - k_f * A + k_r * B,
    D(B, t): k_f * A - k_r * B
}
constraints = {
    GreaterEq(Ea_r, Ea_f)
}

fit = Fit(model, ... , constraints=constraints)
```

* I have been running them with `trial txdbus`, which results in:
  * Python 2.7 on my dev system: `Ran 258 tests in 1.918s` `PASSED (successes=258)`

Hi Kyle,

The use of `self` in this context isn't really what `self` is for, `self` is used internally within a Class to get a reference to an object.

There are several ways of achieving what you want to do.

The root cause of your problem is that you need construct a way of linking each element in the List `colors` to a PushButton object.

You "could" use a dictionary to keep track your PushButton objects. e.g.

```python
def printer(value):
           print(value)
           buttons[value].destroy() #this is where I need "self", to destroy the button that was clicked

colors = ["red", "green", "blue", "purple"]
buttons = {}
i = 0
while i < len(colors):
    color = colors[i]
    buttons[color] = PushButton(app, text=color command=lambda color=color : printer(color)  )
    i += 1
```

I did a few minor modifications to see where the time issues are coming from and it seems that it's not so much the list data structure being used but the comparison between values that is taking up a majority of the time.

For example, on my machine, your perf test clocked in at about 24 seconds. When I replaced:

https://github.com/dgilland/pydash/blob/cb103ca6363cc5d497c116b11534aa18bf177a2f/pydash/arrays.py#L2297

with `if cmp_item == seen_item:`, then the time dropped to about 1.8 seconds. I tracked other inefficiencies down to `pydash.is_equal|is_equal_with`, but optimizing those didn't have as big of an impact. Even replacing:

https://github.com/dgilland/pydash/blob/cb103ca6363cc5d497c116b11534aa18bf177a2f/pydash/arrays.py#L2287

with `comparator = lambda a, b: a == b` took about 8 seconds.

Replacing:

https://github.com/dgilland/pydash/blob/cb103ca6363cc5d497c116b11534aa18bf177a2f/pydash/arrays.py#L2297

with:

```python
if comparator is None:
    was_seen = cmp_item == seen_item
else:
    was_seen = comparator(cmp_item, seen_item)

if was_seen:
    new = False
    break
```

yielded about 3.5 seconds.

After another pass-through, I was about to get the base case back down to about 1 second with the following implementation:

```python
def iterunique(array, comparator=None, iteratee=None):
    """Yield each unique item in array."""
    if not array:  # pragma: no cover
        return

    if iteratee is not None:
        iteratee = pyd.iteratee(iteratee)

    seen = []
    for item in array:
        if iteratee is None:
            cmp_item = item
        else:
            cmp_item = iteratee(item)

        if comparator is None:
            new = cmp_item not in seen
        else:
            new = True
            for seen_item in seen:
                if comparator(cmp_item, seen_item):
                    new = False
                    break

        if new:
            yield item
            seen.append(cmp_item)
```

Assuming that change doesn't break any tests, I can make that a part of the next release which should help improve the time complexity without losing any functionality (namely the customize comparator callback).

@colemickens any opinion on that?

> I expect the token identifies the username. 

the token actually does identify the username, it's just that for some reason you've found an edge case I did not test for, when creating a project under your own name ☺.

> Unfortunately I don't feel Ok using these tokens which are stored in clear text in git config files. I'll stick to ssh with public/private key identification. 

Those are unrelated, and your rationale is wrong. 

The SSH keys are *only* for doing pure git stuff, fetching the remotes, the branches and the commits.

On the other hand, the Private Key is a convenience to grant a tool (such as *git-repo*) control over what it can do with your github or gitlab accounts (for bitbucket, there's no privatekeys and it's still storing login+password which is *wrong*).

The difference between a private key and your full account credentials, is that it's designed to make it hard — if not impossible — to take over an account using only the private key. And also, make it easy to revoke a key (given that you've lost your laptop, phone… that had such a key).

The difference between your private key, and the public/private key scheme of SSH is that the private key is a shared secret given to you by github once you're authenticated using your real credentials (and 2FA if neabled), whereas you give github your public key to authenticate yourself when doing git operations. But in both cases, you do have a cleartext version of a secret (the SSH private key in `~/.ssh/id_rsa` or the shared secret token).

That being said, storing secrets in `~/.gitconfig` is debatable and there's a ticket, #63 about that issue. As a temporary patch, if you refuse to put secrets in `~/.gitconfig` you can set your keys using environment variables:

``` bash
export PRIVATE_KEY_GITHUB=XXXX
export PRIVATE_KEY_GITLAB=YYYY
export PRIVATE_KEY_BITBUCKET=login:password
git hub create test
```

Okay so I did some troubleshooting and I think I figured *some* stuff.

The `path` parameter or the PyTessBaseAPI seems to be overriden by the environnement variable TESSDATA_PREFIX, even if said var is an empty string.

For example, both
    api = PyTessBaseAPI()

and

    api = PyTessBaseAPI(path='/usr/local/') 

will return `RuntimeError: Failed to init API, possibly an invalid tessdata path?`

While setting the environnement variable to `/usr/local/` will make it work (if that's where you tessdata/ folder is).

Hi roinaveiro,

Thanks for raising this issue. Your understanding is correct, but do note that the underlying assumption is different here between pydlm and the R code.

Based on Page 53 of the book, what the R code doing is similar to pydlm with a key difference that it assumes the observation noise (v_t) is fixed to be 1. While in pydlm, the observation noise (noisePrior) is initialized at 1 but then it will be learned from the data and changing over time (You can tell this from the parameter name). Therefore, the result you get from R will be different from pydlm due to this self-learning property.

One way to get a consistent result using pydlm is to just fix the observation noise or set the initial confidence on the noisePrior to be high so the noise value won't get updated. However, this parameter is current not exposed to the user. Please do let me know if you think it is an important feature to you (i.e., tell the model not to learn observational noise from the data).

Thanks!

A particular `Client` instance can not be `run` more than once.

This is because of a few particular details about how the `Client` operates. For reference, a typical `Client` lifecycle goes something like this:
- Upon instantiation, the `Client` acquires an event loop from either the `loop` kwarg or the default event loop for the current thread. A connection state and HTTP client is created as well as flags indicating what part in the lifecycle the `Client` is in.
- Upon being `run`, the `Client` begins running the event loop, during which it logins into Discord and begins listening to the websocket (which it continues doing indefinitely until it is interrupted).
- Upon the client closing, flags are set to indicate the client is no longer running, pending tasks on the event loop are cancelled and any exceptions raised, and finally the event loop is closed.

The error you're experiencing is directly related to the last point - the event loop the `Client` has established itself upon is no longer usable, all of its tasks have been cancelled and it has been closed.

Even if we were able to replace the event loop, many integral parts of the client such as its `ConnectionState` and `HTTPClient` hold hard references back to the old event loop and it still wouldn't function correctly, and even if we could replace all references, the client has established through its lifecycle state that it is closed and no longer usable.

This leaves one sensible option - which is to recreate our `Client` instance entirely on a new event loop, or in other words to do something like this:
```py
while True:
    client = discord.Client()
    ...
    client.run(...)

    asyncio.set_event_loop(asyncio.new_event_loop())  # create a new default event loop so it is fresh for next time
```

This rectifies all of our issues by starting with a clean slate on a new event loop every time, so we don't have stale references or confusing states.

It should also be mentioned that if the purpose of this while loop is merely to restart the bot application should it fail or exit such that it runs constantly, that there are there are various service/process managers available like `systemd` services or `openrc` that can help keep the script running without any extra code bloat.

Removing: Set "`default=None, required=False`" on a column you want to remove, slowly remove old data. When all old data is out, remove the column from the model. Also done this dozens of times. Works pretty well.

> session mgmt for perf

Fewer round-trips = better, generally. So the current session manager keeps changes until you do "`session.commit()`" You can disable this, and all caching, with documented defaults. Check the docs in English here: http://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#using-a-non-caching-session-object

> lower level integration with rom/redis

How low-level do you want to go?

> My Thoughts:
> 
> Adding is covered with rom.util.refresh_indices, how about removing/altering.

It is. Also, as an alternate interface: http://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#rom.util.clean_old_index

> Any concern? rom.util.refresh_cols with default value?

Yeah, that might be funky. I'd wait to add a "`default=val`` if `val != None`, until you'd backfilled the data.

> This is covered with rom.util.Session, I will unit test it today.

Mostly, yes.

> I believe rom is sqlalchemy orm, and redis-py is sqlalchemy core for now. As before go through rom source code there is no clue about key pattern and data dependency for rom. I think I should go through source code first, and any reference?

Rom is basically the applied implementation of chapter 7 of my book, minus the ad stuff. Indeed, it is meant to cover many/most use-cases of SQLAlchemy ORM, inside Redis, minus joins. Though foreign key constraints *are* held, and the constraints are also used as indexes, like a traditional database. Redis is a database construction kit, and I've just built a database minus aggregations (missing a query language, for now) on top of it. Mostly data is stored a json dump of data. You can have sub-object columns (json object columns storing lists of things, indexed in certain ways), but not direct access to underlying structures themselves.

Unlike other Redis database interfaces, we generally don't offer direct access to intersections, unions, differences, etc., but build higher-level concepts on top of them. Data usage is generally moderate to high, and some queries on large sets can be slow; but that's a Redis problem, not a rom problem.

At some point it may make sense to re-implement with modules in mind, but if I can't pay someone to host a Redis module, I'm not interested in implementing to that spec, and I can't expect anyone else to either. I like the idea, I just think modules should be in Lua (or at least not C/C++ due to the build process nightmares I've personally experienced).

If you want to see how data maps between Python and Redis, check: https://github.com/josiahcarlson/rom/blob/master/rom/columns.py

If you want to see how data is indexed/queried, check: https://github.com/josiahcarlson/rom/blob/master/rom/index.py and https://github.com/josiahcarlson/rom/blob/master/rom/query.py , respectively.

> At last thanks again. If I would like recommend a book about Redis with python for application in real world, Redis in Action is the best option or might be the only option for now +1

I am happy to have made something that helps solve problems. If you are planning on using this in your corporation, you may want to consider hiring me at least temporarily as a consultant. Not only will you get private (instead of public support), I can give you possibly the best advice on how to scale your Redis infrastructure, as well as the data operations you will be performing (whether with rom or some other Redis package). My email address is on my public Github profile.

Let me know if I can answer any more questions.

I was able to do something like this using an alternate utils.collate_line_layout. 

    def collate_line_layout(line_chars, tolerance=0, line_width=612, fixed_width=90):
        tolerance = decimalize(tolerance)
        space_width =  decimalize ((line_width + 0.0) / fixed_width )
        coll = ""
        last_x1 = 0
        for char in sorted(line_chars, key=itemgetter("x0")):
            if last_x1 != None and char["x0"] > (last_x1 + tolerance):
                delta_x = char["x0"] - last_x1
                num_spaces = int(round( delta_x / space_width ))
                if num_spaces == 0: 
                    num_spaces = 1
                coll += " " * num_spaces
            last_x1 = char["x1"]
            coll += char["text"]
        return coll

if url is None:

Hey László,

The "compat" mode exists so that all (supported) types can be dumped to JSON. For successful reconstruction, the decoding side has to put in a little bit of extra effort. For example, dates are serialized to plain ISO8601 strings, without the information about the type. Currently, `Money` is serialized as a plain `str` in compat mode.

Regarding the `djmoney.money.Money` class - I'm reluctant in putting that in the generic `jsonplus` package. It would probably make more sense to put Django-specific stuff to `django-jsonplus` package. I'll look into that. Btw, I see `django-money` already has some serialization support for Django -- have you tried using it?

You didn't miss anything.  Changing seasons has always proven to be a bit problematic.

I'm not positive on this, but I think this mostly has to do with the fact that - by design - the schedule is not rebuilt every time `update_sched` is run unless explicitly told to do so.  Only the [current week](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/17f2f866302383adaef6c73b00483ddf64263e6c/nflgame/sched.py#L42) is updated by default if necessary.  So if you miss week(s) at the start of a season those weeks are not attempted to be updated unless told to specifically with `--year ####`.  

Not sure if it would resolve this entirely, but in addition to [`last_updated`](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/17f2f866302383adaef6c73b00483ddf64263e6c/nflgame/sched.py#L33) being updated/checked in `schedule.json` maybe we could also include there the schedule URL that was last used to update the schedule.  If a different URL was used this time than last time, then instead of only updating the week, the entire current year would be updated.

I made the following changes to my local install and both nflgame and nfldb work fine.

The database is keyed off the team code so updating it in the team table automatically propagates the change to dependent keys. The following statement addresses that:
`update team set team_id = 'JAX' where team_id = 'JAC';`

Updating the following entry in nfldb/team.py and nflgame/__init__.py:
`['JAC', 'Jacksonville', 'Jaguars', 'Jacksonville Jaguars', 'JAX'],`
to
`['JAX', 'Jacksonville', 'Jaguars', 'Jacksonville Jaguars', 'JAC'],`
addresses the rest of the required changes.

The filter's default setting for "and", "or" and "to" can be modified by providing a [`language`](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-filter.html#localization) setting:

```js
$.extend($.tablesorter.language, {
  to  : '-',
  or  : '\|', // escape any special regexp characters, for now
  and : '&&'
});
```

The settings must not include surrounding spaces, and can not be an empty string.

In the next update, I'll catch the above problems to:
* Remove surrounding spaces
* Work around empty strings. 
* Automatically escape special regular expression characters.

Related: https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/issues/1089#issuecomment-164088751 & https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/issues/1101

Hi @DucVuMinh

I'm sorry for the lack of rigurosity. The idea behind using a random initialized vector, with values under the same distribution of the known words, for unknown words is that you will get a point in the vector space that looks like a real observed word and therefore you will be able to operate or apply all the distance calculations consistently with the known words.

I co-authored a paper at IJCAI2017 (https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2017/573) where we used a similar idea when assigning vectors to words we want to replace. (Basically we wanted to turn question sentences into something that looks like statements where the vectors representing the wh-question words (who/when/where) were replaced by the vectors of words that are most likely to make the sentence "similar" (under a given metric) to the answer sentence for each question type).

Anyway, I think it is a somewhat-common trick to build vectors with random values (under the same distribution of the already known words) in order to initialize the vectors for unknown words and then make them fit your training dataset to represent the averaged distribution of the unknown words in your data. I'm not sure right now about exactly which other papers present this idea, but it is a technical tip shared mostly everywhere I can remember, I think if you google it you will find several results (answers at stackexchange.com, blogs, other repositories). As of version 0.2.0 of Augmentor, it is now possible to pass mask images or ground truth images with original images and have them augmented in the same, identical way. The README and Read the Docs contain more information, but briefly if you initialise a `Pipeline` object in the normal way, you have the option of specifying a *ground truth* directory using the `ground_truth()` function:

```python
p = Augmentor.Pipeline("/path/to/images")

# Specify a directory containing the ground truth or mask images:
p.ground_truth("/path/to/ground_truth_images")

# Add operations to the pipeline as normal:
p.rotate(probability=1, max_left_rotation=5, max_right_rotation=5)

# Sample as normal:
p.sample(1000)
```
Hope this helps.

Each insert statement will generate one resultset (containing the row count), which you are not consuming. SQL Server will block processing of further statements in a batch, if there are pending resultsets filling up its write buffer. Try this instead:

```
        with pyodbc.connect(connection_string) as con:
            cursor = con.cursor()
            cursor.execute('\n'.join(insert_statements))
            while cursor.nextset():
                pass
            cursor.commit()
```

 Please see https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/827575/sql-server-does-not-finish-execution-of-a-large-batch-of-sql-statement for more information.

1) The `listOfProperties` parameter of a `WriteAccessSpecification` is a sequence of `PropertyValue`, not a `PropertyReference`.

2) After you parse the value, it needs to go into the `PropertyValue` like `prop_value.value = value`, it's not a parameter of the request.

3) It looks like you are missing the priority.

It might make it simpler to understand if you parse the parameters in the same order as they are in the request, i.e., property identifier followed by the optional array index, then the value, then optionally the priority.  Once you are happy with the way parsing a `PropertyValue` is working, you can nest that in a loop for more than one of them, then parse the object identifier off the front and build a `WriteAccessSpecification`, then loop around that for multiple objects.

@audreyr There are a few possible ways to fix this:

1. change the heuristics to treat bytes below and above 127 differently. Use some new heuristic based on the proportion of high bytes to declare a file as binary. 

2. keep the heuristics as-is and introduce chardet as a dependency. If a file is detected as binary in certain cases (excluding the null byte detection), apply a second level detection using `chardet` to see if an encoding can be guessed with a high enough confidence (say above 0.8). If so try to convert the buffer to unicode using the detected encoding. If this fails the file is binary, else the file is text.

3. mix approach 1 and 2, using 2. only when high bytes are found.

* Create some sample images with different width/height/dpi.
* Insert them into Excel files.
* Convert them into test files [like this](https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/blob/master/xlsxwriter/test/comparison/test_header_image01.py):.
* Write some parse code to read the width/height/dpi from the file.
* Ensure that the tests pass and the resulting file is the same as the Excel file.

The format will only be lost if the format isn't referenced anywhere else in the applications code.
So then the names are definitively **NOT** unique. And thus the `name` parameter is definitively **NOT** like a dictionary key. If you would like people to think of the format name as a key in a dictionary of formats, then the names really must be unique. Otherwise, you are asking for subtle bugs. Inevitably, someone (or multiple someones working on the same program) will unintentionally create two formats with the same name, and some parts of their code will reference one and other parts will reference the other. It won't be obvious why cells with the same format name have different attributes in the finished workbook. Or someone will try to modify one format object but unwittingly modify a different format object instead (even though they used the correct name!), and *really* be confused by the output. What you are proposing goes against this passage in your docs, if people think of format names as a proxy for format references:
It isn’t possible to use a Format with a write() method and then redefine it for use at a later stage. This is because a Format is applied to a cell not in its current state but in its final state.
Really, if names are to be thought of as keys in a dictionary, then why not just make them **actual** keys in a dictionary? What is the use case for having multiple formats with the same name? Even supposing there is one, I would be very surprised if its utility is worth breaking the simple, straightforward, intuitive name-as-proxy-for-reference mental model. Note that the latter part of OP's proposal even uses the term "reference" when talking about the format name!

@nishadhka there is an intrinsic problem with this and this led to the proposed changes not being included in the library: It is not possible to map the group transformations onto attributes. A simple example would be the line width of a circle wich you stretch along the x axis by a factor of two: if the thickness attribute of the path is two, the resulting ellipse after the group transformation will have a line width varying from two to four along the path, because the object is first created and then transformed. This can't be mapped on a flat list of paths but would need some Python implementation of SVG groups which is a lot of work to do and will probably cause problems if you want to use the path objects with other libraries.

If you are only interested in the path itself, one can use the fix I proposed. If you need the attributes such as line width to be transformed as well, this will not do the job for you. If you only need the path itself, please tell me, then I will provide you an updated fork of this library which you can use.

This is more than just a consistency thing. Because the WSGI script uses sys.path.extend it means that buildout-installed dependencies get overridden by system-wide packages, and this makes djangorecipe-based deployments hard or impossible in shared hosting situations.

As an example, my particular situation is a host with lxml 1.3 installed but a deployment that needs lxml 2.2. The 2.2 egg is installed just fine, but because the WSGI script extends the path the systemwide 1.3 egg is always loaded instead.

After starting to use PyEnv myself on a new mac that I setup with powerline. @b-ryan's comment gave me an idea. I had installed powerline using the pyenv installed python and so there was a shim present at `~/.pyenv/shims/powerline`. I removed the pyenv version and installed system wide using `sudo` which placed it in `/usr/local/bin/powerline`. 

When I then activated my (pyenv) environment I no longer got the error. I believe it's because pyenv places it's shim folder in front of your `$PATH` and then since you are in a new environment with the shim references having priority but no powerline in the env you end up with the "command not found" error. 

Try removing powerline from the pyenv and installing it system wide first before activating.

Arik,

We've had issues with Amazon instance capacity as well. As far as I know, there is no public insight into this; AWS' [Troubleshooting Instance Capacity page, for the InsufficientInstanceCapacity error](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/instance-capacity.html#instance-capacity-insufficient) basically says to wait a while, request fewer instances, try a different AZ, or try a different instance size. RightScale has some [docs on it too](http://docs.rightscale.com/faq/clouds/aws/How_come_I_get_an_insufficient_capacity_error_when_launching_an_EC2_instance.html), which basically say the same thing. There's definitely no API to find this out (especially since Amazon tends to consider their actual capacity and how they manage it to be a secret), and I haven't found this exposed in any sort of status page or API.

From what I can tell, this isn't really considered an error condition by AWS, at least not enough to merit reporting it publicly on a status page. We know that AWS (from the little they talk about what they do behind the scenes) is really big on utilizing their infrastructure resources as much as possible. I can only assume that they have very little (comparatively speaking, very little in terms of their scale) in the way of idle compute resources, and power on or provision new hardware essentially as-needed. Hence every once in a while, demand will increase quickly and there's a bit of a delay before resources are available. Anecdotally, most of the times I've seen this happen in us-east-1 have been around 8-11 AM Eastern, when traffic jumps.

The "real" answer at least according to Amazon's best practices and what some of their massive-scale customers say, is that apps should be architected to be at least multi-AZ, if not multi-region, and that you shouldn't rely on a particular AZ having capacity any more than you should rely on a particular AZ being working correctly.

I agree that these aren't "good" answers, and I wish they provided more insight into the inner workings and how much capacity is available where, but I can agree with the experts that from a reliability perspective, none of us should be relying on a single AZ working correctly or having capacity.

CMSPLUGIN_CASCADE_PLUGINS = [

]

The drawback of the current approach is that users get tables for plugins they do not use. But that is the lesser evil, compared to shipping an app with incomplete migrations.

The good thing about discarding this PR is that we do not have to come up with a name for the migration ;)

```
import cv2
import numpy as np
from compiler.ast import flatten

def calculate_pHash(img1, img2):
    """
        缩小图片：32 * 32是一个较好的大小，这样方便DCT计算
        转化为灰度图：把缩放后的图片转化为256阶的灰度图。（具体算法见平均哈希算法步骤）
        计算DCT:DCT把图片分离成分率的集合
        缩小DCT：DCT计算后的矩阵是32 * 32，保留左上角的8 * 8，这些代表的图片的最低频率
        计算平均值：计算缩小DCT后的所有像素点的平均值。
        进一步减小DCT：大于平均值记录为1，反之记录为0.
        得到信息指纹：组合64个信息位，顺序随意保持一致性。
    :param img: 图片
    :return: hash值
    """
    image1 = cv2.resize(img1,(32,32))
    image2 = cv2.resize(img2,(32,32))
    gray1 = cv2.cvtColor(image1,cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
    gray2 = cv2.cvtColor(image2,cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
    # 将灰度图转为浮点型，再进行dct变换
    dct1 = cv2.dct(np.float32(gray1))
    dct2 = cv2.dct(np.float32(gray2))
    # 取左上角的8*8，这些代表图片的最低频率
    # 在python中进行掩码操作，可以直接这样取出图像矩阵的某一部分
    dct1_roi = dct1[0:8,0:8]
    dct2_roi = dct2[0:8,0:8]
    hash1 = get_hash(dct1_roi)
    hash2 = get_hash(dct2_roi)
    return Hamming_distance(hash1,hash2)
```

from PIL import Image, ImageDraw

# This is an example of running face recognition on a single image
# and drawing a box around each person that was identified.

# Load a sample picture and learn how to recognize it.
obama_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("obama.jpg")
obama_face_encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(obama_image)[0]

# Load a second sample picture and learn how to recognize it.
biden_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("biden.jpg")
biden_face_encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(biden_image)[0]

# Create arrays of known face encodings and their names
known_face_encodings = [
    obama_face_encoding,
    biden_face_encoding
]
known_face_names = [
    "Barack Obama",
    "Joe Biden"
]

# Load an image with an unknown face
unknown_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("two_people.jpg")

# Find all the faces and face encodings in the unknown image
face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(unknown_image)
face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(unknown_image, face_locations)

# Convert the image to a PIL-format image so that we can draw on top of it with the Pillow library
# See http://pillow.readthedocs.io/ for more about PIL/Pillow
pil_image = Image.fromarray(unknown_image)
# Create a Pillow ImageDraw Draw instance to draw with
draw = ImageDraw.Draw(pil_image)

# Loop through each face found in the unknown image
for (top, right, bottom, left), face_encoding in zip(face_locations, face_encodings):
    # See if the face is a match for the known face(s)
    matches = face_recognition.compare_faces(known_face_encodings, face_encoding)

    name = "Unknown"

    # If a match was found in known_face_encodings, just use the first one.
    if True in matches:
        first_match_index = matches.index(True)
        name = known_face_names[first_match_index]

    # Draw a box around the face using the Pillow module
    draw.rectangle(((left, top), (right, bottom)), outline=(0, 0, 255))

    # Draw a label with a name below the face
    text_width, text_height = draw.textsize(name)
    draw.rectangle(((left, bottom - text_height - 10), (right, bottom)), fill=(0, 0, 255), outline=(0, 0, 255))
    draw.text((left + 6, bottom - text_height - 5), name, fill=(255, 255, 255, 255))


# Remove the drawing library from memory as per the Pillow docs
del draw

# Display the resulting image
pil_image.show()

I recently checked out blending, to get it all right, and what I think was going on, is that you have used a multiplied colors (meaning that the rgb colors are already multiplied with alpha), and used for blending `blendSrc: SrcAlphaFactor` insteaf of `OneFactor`, which makes the WebGL pileline multilpy it again. A better solution is to always (well, mostly) use pre multiplied colors + `OneFactor`.

The safest option is to stop asciimatics, close your Screen while the test is running and then open a new one after it has finished.  However, that means you need to reconstruct where you were before you closed the Screen.  You could re-use the resize infrastructure inside asciimatics to handle that - i.e. save your current Scene and use that as the start_scene when you re-open.

The other options get quite fragile after that...  Assuming you're running on Linux (or another curses system), you could just stop the Screen from playing temporarily - e.g. raise a StopApplication exception - and then use curses directly to switch between the normal and alternate screens, using the `smcup` and `rmcup` terminfo strings.  See http://invisible-island.net/xterm/xterm.faq.html#xterm_tite for some background on what they mean.

If you're in Windows, then you need to remember the current screen buffer and console input, restore the original screen buffer and console input that asciimatics stored away on start-up, and reset them as needed afterwards.  

These latter 2 options use varying amounts of the internals of asciimatics, though, which will likely break in the future.

The other option that springs to mind is to prevent the need for dropping out at all.  You could instead pipe stdout to your asciimatics app, save it off in a widget and then display that instead.

You're welcome.  Have a look at http://asciimatics.readthedocs.io/en/latest/troubleshooting.html#i-can-t-run-it-inside-pycharm-or-other-ides.  If you can configure IDLE to start python using the command shell, please let me know so I can update the docs.

Little note about Python, it would have been better to have 

    elif sys.platform == 'darwin':

Rather than

    elif sys.platform in ('darwin'):

because you are actually writing `'darwin' in 'darwin'` which is a bit silly - but I don't mind - the `in` keyword when the right hand side is a string does an fast substring match which will return True in this case but is unnecessary. What is really happening is `sys.platform` is the string `'darwin'` and `('darwin')` isn't a tuple it's just `'darwin'` with brackets around it, you need the `,` to make a tuple e.g.

    ('I'm a tuple with this sting in it',)
    ('I'm not a tuple im just a string in brackets which are stripped out by the parser')

also:

    (1 + 2,) == (3,)
    (1 + 2)  == 3

The reason why I think this code is not idea is that the additional complexity may be misleading and cause someone to waste time assuming more is going on than is really.

Regardless thanks for the patch!!

In `requirements.txt`, which is parsed by `setup.py`, add `setuptools>=35.0.2`. It's been 5 months since I've had this problem, so I don't remember the exact error, but using a 3.5 virtual environment with a manually installed setuptools of less than 35 should consistently yield the problem. If you wanted to specify the requirement *only* for Python 3.5 and up, you could add `setuptools>=35.02;python_version>="3.4"` to `requirements.txt`. There isn’t any hard and fast rules for this, it mostly depends on the needs of your application. For our use case, we had a few actions that we only wanted to allow if the user had a fresh access token, such as submitting a payment or modifying a dns record. We added that second login endpoint for these actions as needed (we use a low token expires time, and all tokens created with the refresh token are marked as non-fresh), to prevent creating lots of refresh tokens like you said. 

There is no reason you have you use this pattern, or if you wanted you could roll both of those login endpoints into one endpoint with an optional quest string arg to specify if a refresh token should be created as well, or something like that.

This is because Github annoyingly changed their specs for Github Flavored Markdown without warning: http://stackoverflow.com/a/24576087.  Originally this behavior was not confusing and was actually quite accurate.  I don't know why they would do this as it messes up legacy readme formatting.  Sigh...

Anyways, this puts me in a similar annoying position in order to monkey the Github "feel", I have to do the same thing to others who use this extension.   I need to think about this.  I wonder if it would be better to add a deprecation warning about nl2br before we straight up remove it and mess up rendering of peoples' current documentation without warning.

The github extension is just a convenience extension; there is nothing stopping people from individually including and configuring the same extensions.

I am open to discussion, but I always feel it may anger people to have the github extension without warning rendering their documents in a new format.

from marshmallow import fields, Schema

```
from marshmallow import fields, Schema

I think this boils down to the fact that flaky has to do some "interesting" things to work with pytest.

flaky fudges the outcome of tests it wants to retry. pytest believes that an outcome can only be 'passed' or 'failed' (or a few others like skipped), and we must report one of those. So flaky reports a pass for a retry.

There's also a test status, which is a 3-tuple of outcome, 1-letter status, and word status (eg, `('passed', '.', 'PASSED')`). If that's empty, then it's ignored py pytest. This allows us to avoid inflating the count of tests that were run.


The way pytest does this is to call the `pytest_report_teststatus` hook from its `pytest_runtest_logreport` hook. It then uses the resulting status to categorize the test. If the teamcity messages took a similar approach, we'd be compatible.

Otherwise, perhaps flaky's architecture is fundamentally flawed for pytest. Pytest doesn't really have the notion of a 'retry' - that's why flaky exists. As a result, we're stuck with either reporting a fake pass, or with rewriting large parts of the pytest runner inside flaky.

To add to my previous comment; I think it would be ideal if the serialization behavior of both the stride order and byte order would be configurable. From a serialization-purity perspective, we would like to restore the exact byteorder and strides that we put in in the first place; but for practical intents and purposes, it is typically much more desirable to take the opportunity to 'normalize' these attributes to their numpy/platform defaults while we have the chance. There are arguments to be made for both, and I don't think this choice should be forced at the library level. Would be nice if in the constructor we could specify byteorder and strideorder as either relative to platform default, or absolute. But it will require some tinkering.

Another question; should the binary format be explicitly stored? Already ran into this problem when applying jsonpickle, when changing from bz2 to zlib compression... would also make it easier to support npz format as well.

By the way, I realize I havnt really given any thought yet to object arrays; they are fundamentally rather different from a serialization perspective, since they contain references. I suppose these should always be serialized as text... which is fine since they are rarely very big anyway... Ill make some tests for it.

When a Cypher error occurs, the transaction is rolled back by the server and therefore the client will be unable to subsequently roll back that transaction itself. Conversely, a client-side exception is unseen by the server and the client will need to explicitly perform a rollback.

You probably want to consider catching `CypherError` instead of just general exceptions so that you can distinguish between the two.

Since the inability to pickle function objects is a well-known limitation of `pickle`/`cPickle`, how would the community feel about just requiring the [`dill` module ](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dill) as a dependency of `PySparkTask`? A simple `import dill as pickle` in both `pyspark_runner.py` and `spark.py` would very likely resolve this issue.

The way the Task is currently designed I think it would be unnecessary overhead to add a dedicated `exists` check. The ExtractTask asserts to have a singleton `BigQueryTarget` as requires (which's exists method is essentially the `table_exists`). The main reason behind is separation responsibility. The ExtractTask should take care of unloading the table while it is luigi's job to take care of dependency checking and resolution. 
In case the table is non-existent at run-time it will result in a BigQuery exception which I think is appropriate behavior.

There is no real concept of logging in / logging out. The keys are used to sign your request. AWS then uses its copies of those keys to verify the request. This happens for every request.

Now you can sort of emulate logging in by using [Assume Role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_AssumeRole.html).  With that you have a set of credentials that you use to 'login' for a period of time. You get back a new set of credentials that expire in some amount of time (typically one hour).

This is quite typical problem with dynamic web applications. Usually it is bets to ask this type of question in the support channels: https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary#support 

But here are some pointers:
1) Wait for a element which you know that is loaded last in the page by using the [Wait Until Page Contains Element](http://robotframework.org/SeleniumLibrary/SeleniumLibrary.html#Wait%20Until%20Page%20Contains%20Element)
2) Sometimes there is loading indicator in the page, therefore wait for the loading indicator to appear and then wait to [disappear](http://robotframework.org/SeleniumLibrary/SeleniumLibrary.html#Wait%20Until%20Element%20Is%20Not%20Visible). 
3) The 40s wait sounds really long one, perhaps you have some sort of performance problem in your web application. Or speed up the page load with some other means, this is usually best to discuss with your developers. 
4) My app runs on JQuery, so I poll JQuery.active to return zero two times in a row before I allow interactions with the page. 
5) Discuss with your development, how to better handle long timeouts. You and your development are the best persons who know how your application works and therefore best persons to answer your question. 

But my keywords are full of waiting, polling and retry's, because that it how modern web applications are   build and that is the only way to make test stable.

> @eli-schwartz click handles much of the overhead so it saves us from having to be experts on this specific thing most of the time

Yes, but I sort of wish click generated proper completions. :smiley: There *are* tools which generate "proper" completions  thereby saving every application developer the effort of knowing how it works.

> @eli-schwartz though this doesn’t stay up-to-date when you upgrade. i have far too many things in my completions to remember to re-export their completion script every time up upgrade.

But it does. The current completion simply executes pipenv every time you try to complete anything; this doesn't need to be checked for updates to the completion definition...


Either way it does not stop the completion from being delivered in `data_files` to `share/bash-completion/completions`, which should work, and be upgraded, whether installed via `sudo pip` (:cry:), `pip install --user` (since https://github.com/scop/bash-completion/commit/1d25d72ca8633c19cb089dff447d08c531379c59 it will be loaded from `~/.local`), or `python setup.py install --root="$package_manager_install_root"`.

This would save on one initial call to pipenv on every single new interactive shell, which while not quite as significant as one call on every single `pipenv <TAB>` is still not insignificant. It's why bash-completion created the idea of lazy-loaded completions and tries to encourage people to use that over the "COMPAT_DIR" in `/etc/bash_completion.d`.

> you should not assume that people don’t know much at all about thing x. it’s better to assume that their workflow, use-case, or tolerance is different to yours.

In the ideal case, users do *not* know, because it is automagically handled by developers or distro packagers. For example, https://git.archlinux.org/svntogit/community.git/commit/trunk?h=packages/python-pipenv&id=484eec9eabad806654fea29801577dc2cbe4188a

So the problem seems to be that `easy_install`, which is invoked by `python setup.py develop` to install the package and its dependencies, mistakenly thinks one of the dependency packages, `certifi`, can stay zipped after getting installed, and still work.

Unfortunately, it seems `requests` tries to access a resource from this package, assuming it's a directory (unzipped), which throws the `invalid path` error.

One solution might be using

```
$ pip install -e .
```

instead of

```
python setup.py develop
```

to install package in dev mode, since `pip` seems to always unzip packages per https://stackoverflow.com/a/2090124/1797347!

Release v0.5.3 with msgpack_kwargs support in `FluentHandler`.
No need to access internal attribute.

```
import os
import logging
from fluent import handler

FLUENT_HOST = os.getenv('FLUENT_HOST', '127.0.0.1')
FLUENT_PORT = int(os.getenv('FLUENT_PORT', 24224))
FLUENT_TAG = os.getenv('FLUENT_TAG', 'test.log')

def encode_exception(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, Exception):
        return {"class":type(obj).__name__, "args":obj.args}
    return obj

if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    logger = logging.getLogger('fluent.test')
    h = handler.FluentHandler(FLUENT_TAG, host=FLUENT_HOST, port=FLUENT_PORT, msgpack_kwargs={'default' : encode_exception}, verbose=True)
    formatter = handler.FluentRecordFormatter()
    h.setFormatter(formatter)
    logger.addHandler(h)

    logger.info(Exception('foo'))
```

This is not a trivial problem. What's happening is that verification doesn't take [special method lookup](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-lookup) into account. How *should* interfaces handle special methods, like `__add__` and `__contains__`? These methods can never be directly provided, really. And the fact that Python 3.7 adds support for modules [directly providing `__dir__` and `__getattr__`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0562/) is going to make special-casing of special methods more complicated.

And for classes without a more specific metaclass than `type`, it could be argued that they could only ever "directly provide" `__call__(*args, **kwargs)`, never something more specific. That the `type.__call__()` method used for `ClassObj()` invokes `__new__` and, optionally, `__new__`, is an implementation detail that `verifyObject` can't be expected to handle.

@karesh did you set `'JWT_ALLOW_REFRESH': True,` in settings of your app?

The current solution requires us to use one of the following strategies :

 - get jwt token with `username` and `password`
 - store this token on client side
 - refresh this token every 5 minutes

The user close the app for 5 minutes > login again (not acceptable for an UX perspective)

To move forward, here the [section](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-6) in the OAuth 2.0 RFC, talking about the refreshing an Access Token : 

> Because refresh tokens are typically long-lasting credentials used to
   request additional access tokens, the refresh token is bound to the
   client to which it was issued.

Its a encoding issue, i think..

Just try this out.
open setup.py and change these lines below:
```python
curdir = dirname(__file__)
with open(join(curdir, "README.rst")) as fd:
    readme = fd.read()
with open(join(curdir, "CHANGELOG.md")) as fd:
    changelog = fd.read()
```

Change it to this : 
```python
curdir = dirname(__file__)
with open(join(curdir, "README.rst"),encoding='utf8') as fd:
    readme = fd.read()
with open(join(curdir, "CHANGELOG.md"),encoding='utf8') as fd:
    changelog = fd.read()
```

And try it out.

I believe I have committed two mistakes due to misunderstanding from my part.
First, I have referred to pythonforandroid/recipes/*/ directories in requirements sources sections, not the original packages. Of course it makes no sense at all, it was my lack of grasping.
Second, I assumed that parsing buildozer.spec file would skip any part after '#' even after a statement in the same line, like in python code and shell script, which actually wasn't the case. I had commented inline in requirements section (I omitted in my post since I thought they have no effect), but moving the commented requirement to another line let me build successfully.
Hence I am closing this false-issue, since it was only my fault, and I would like to apologize for anyone who spent a minute trying to find out the issue.

OK, I figured out that the join operation in @dbieber's version is what was breaking the use of arguments of type `bytes`, by turning them back into strings.

The attached python script (with a `.txt` prefix to keep github happy, but you should rename it `firem`) will load files (with `-f` option) and modules (with `-m` option), and leave bytes strings alone.

[firem.txt](https://github.com/google/python-fire/files/870564/firem.txt)

So now this works: 

```
$ firem -m base64 b85encode "b'Hello world'" - decode                                                                 
NM&qnZy<MXa%^M
$ firem -m base64 b85decode "b'NM&qnZy<MXa%^M'" - decode                                                              
Hello world
```

I don't think that this reasoning is correct.

Look at Section 8.3.3 of SPARQL 1.1 Query Language, at
https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#idp899488.

The example there is

PREFIX : <http://example.com/>
SELECT * WHERE {
        ?x :p ?n
        FILTER NOT EXISTS {
                ?x :q ?m .
                FILTER(?n = ?m)
        }
}

which is analogous to my example and the wording says that ?n is bound in the
inner filter.

On 03/28/2016 03:39 AM, Jörn Hees wrote:
> so you're complaining that the following query doesn't return any results:
> 
> |SELECT ?this WHERE { VALUES ?this { <y> <n> } FILTER (EXISTS { VALUES ?valu {
> <y> <o> } FILTER ( ?this = ?valu ) }) } |
> 
> This is actually by design in SPARQL: The inner-most query is evaluated first.
> 
> The consequence is that we have the following execution order:
> 
> |EXISTS { VALUES ?valu { <y> <o> } FILTER ( ?this = ?valu ) # ?this is an
> unknown variable here } |
> 
> As the |?this| in the above is still unbound the equality always fails, so the
> result of the |EXISTS| is false. Then the following is executed:
> 
> |SELECT ?this WHERE { VALUES ?this { <y> <n> } FILTER (false) } |
> 
> Which correctly doesn't result in a binding for |?this|.
> 
> —

Hi @RachitKansal ,

this doesn't look at first glance like an issue in the lib, but a connection issue. Please note that a project's issue tracker is not a place to look for support, but rather to report bugs/issues with the library.

Having said that, let's see if I can help you with this. As you can see from the error message, there's an SSL level issue. I'd recommend that you try to troubleshoot it without using the Jira library first: try connecting to your Jira instance using your browser. If that works, move back into Python. In my experience, EOF can happen when basic agreement about the protocol can't be reached (for example, your Python version is compiled against an openssl version so old that supports only SSLv3 and the server may be configured to support TLSv1.2; or they do, client and server, support the same protocol version, but there's no overlap between ciphers; etc.). Try a request using Python's requests package (it's what Python's JIRA uses). If that fails, search the web for fixes for that (you'll see options like installing requests[security], pyopenssl and stuff like that).

If everything works from a Python console/script using requests, but fails with the Jira lib, then do open a ticket.

`from __future__ import unicode_literals

class Meta:
    def __init__(self):

class POSTagger():

        self.CHARS_LOOKUP = self.model.add_lookup_parameters((self.meta.n_chars, self.meta.c_dim))

        # MLP on top of biLSTM outputs 100 -> 32 -> ntags
        self.W1 = self.model.add_parameters((self.meta.n_hidden, self.meta.lstm_word_dim*2))
        self.W1C = self.model.add_parameters((self.meta.n_hidden, self.meta.lstm_word_dim*2+self.meta.n_hidden))
        #self.W1C = self.model.add_parameters((self.meta.n_hidden, self.meta.lstm_word_dim*2))
        self.W2 = self.model.add_parameters((self.meta.n_tags, self.meta.n_hidden))
        self.W2C = self.model.add_parameters((self.meta.n_ctags, self.meta.n_hidden))
        self.B1 = self.model.add_parameters(self.meta.n_hidden)
        self.B1C = self.model.add_parameters(self.meta.n_hidden)
        self.B2 = self.model.add_parameters(self.meta.n_tags)
        self.B2C = self.model.add_parameters(self.meta.n_ctags)

        # word-level LSTMs
        self.fwdRNN = dy.LSTMBuilder(1, self.meta.w_dim+self.meta.lstm_char_dim*2, self.meta.lstm_word_dim, self.model) 
        self.bwdRNN = dy.LSTMBuilder(1, self.meta.w_dim+self.meta.lstm_char_dim*2, self.meta.lstm_word_dim, self.model)

        # char-level LSTMs
        self.cFwdRNN = dy.LSTMBuilder(1, self.meta.c_dim, self.meta.lstm_char_dim, self.model)

    def get_index(self, ow):
        idx = None 
        is_url = False
        try:
            return self.meta.w2i[ow]
            if args.lang == 'eng':
                return self.meta.w2i[ow.lower()]
        except KeyError:
            pass
        if ow.translate(self.punct_table).isdigit():
            return self.meta.w2i['N-U-M']
        if (ow.startswith('http://') or ow.startswith('https://') or ow.startswith('www.')):
            is_url = True
        elif self.isurl(ow):
            tokens = self.upunkt.split(ow)
            is_url = any(tk in self.domains for tk in tokens[1:])
        if is_url:
            idx = self.meta.w2i['U-R-L']
        return idx
    
    def word_rep(self, w, test=True):
        if not test and random.random() < 0.25:
            return self.WORDS_LOOKUP[0]
        w_idx = self.get_index(w)
        if w_idx is not None:
            return self.WORDS_LOOKUP[w_idx]
        else:
            return self.WORDS_LOOKUP[0]
    
    def char_rep(self, w, cf_init, cb_init):
        pad_char = self.meta.c2i["<*>"]
        char_ids = [pad_char] + [self.meta.c2i[c] if self.meta.cc[c]>5 else self.meta.c2i['_UNK_'] for c in w] + [pad_char]
        char_embs = [self.CHARS_LOOKUP[cid] for cid in char_ids]
        fw_exps = cf_init.transduce(char_embs)
        bw_exps = cb_init.transduce(reversed(char_embs))
        return dy.concatenate([ fw_exps[-1], bw_exps[-1] ])
    
    def build_tagging_graph(self, words, test=False, tag='pos'):
        dy.renew_cg()
        # parameters -> expressions
        w1 = dy.parameter(self.W1)
        b1 = dy.parameter(self.B1)
        if tag == 'pos':
            w2 = dy.parameter(self.W2)
            b2 = dy.parameter(self.B2)
        else:
            w1c = dy.parameter(self.W1C)
            b1c = dy.parameter(self.B1C)
            w2c = dy.parameter(self.W2C)
            b2c = dy.parameter(self.B2C)
            if not test:
                w1c = dy.dropout(w1c, 0.25)
                b1c = dy.dropout(b1c, 0.25)
        if test:
            self.fwdRNN.disable_dropout()
            self.bwdRNN.disable_dropout()
            self.cFwdRNN.disable_dropout()
            self.cBwdRNN.disable_dropout()
        else:
            w1 = dy.dropout(w1, 0.25)
            b1 = dy.dropout(b1, 0.25)
            self.fwdRNN.set_dropout(0.25)
            self.bwdRNN.set_dropout(0.25)
            self.cFwdRNN.set_dropout(0.25)
            self.cBwdRNN.set_dropout(0.25)
    
        # initialize the RNNs
        f_init = self.fwdRNN.initial_state()
        b_init = self.bwdRNN.initial_state()
    
        cf_init = self.cFwdRNN.initial_state()
        cb_init = self.cBwdRNN.initial_state()
    
        # get the word vectors. word_rep(...) returns a 128-dim vector expression for each word.
        wembs = [self.word_rep(w, test) for w in words]
        cembs = [self.char_rep(w, cf_init, cb_init) for w in words]
        wembs = [dy.concatenate([w,c]) for w,c in zip(wembs, cembs)]
    
        # feed word vectors into biLSTM
        fw_exps = f_init.transduce(wembs)
        bw_exps = b_init.transduce(reversed(wembs))
    
        # biLSTM states
        bi_exps = [dy.concatenate([f,b]) for f,b in zip(fw_exps, reversed(bw_exps))]
    
        # feed each biLSTM state to an MLP
        exps = []
        for x in bi_exps:
            if tag == 'pos':
                r_p = w2*(dy.rectify(w1 * x) + b1) + b2
                exps.append(r_p)    
                continue
            r_c = w2c*(dy.rectify(w1c * dy.concatenate([w1*x+b1, x])) + b1c) + b2c
            exps.append(r_c)
    
        return exps
    
    def sent_loss(self, words, tags, ctags, tag='pos'):
        vecs = self.build_tagging_graph(words, tag=tag)
        errs = []
        for v,t,ct in zip(vecs,tags,ctags):
            if tag == 'pos':
                tid = self.meta.t2i[t]
            else:
                tid = self.meta.ct2i[ct]
            err = dy.pickneglogsoftmax(v, tid)
            errs.append(err)
        return dy.esum(errs)
    
    def tag_sent(self, words, tag='pos'):
        p_vecs = self.build_tagging_graph(words, test=True, tag=tag)
        p_vecs = [dy.softmax(v) for v in p_vecs]
        p_probs = [v.npvalue() for v in p_vecs]
        tags, ctags = [], []
        for p_prb in p_probs:
            xtag = np.argmax(p_prb)
            if tag == 'pos':
                tags.append(self.meta.i2t[xtag])
            else:
                tags.append(self.meta.i2ct[xtag])
        return zip(words, tags)

def read(fname):
    data = []
    sent = []

    fp = io.open(fname, encoding='utf-8')

    if sent: data.append(sent)
    return data

In the manuscript there were other concerns about the 'default' state of a model:

> What if the reconstruction refers to a species which cannot be isolated or cultivated? A model might not be initialized with environmental conditions if they are unknown. Yet such model might still be perfectly valid and useful (eg: for EFM analysis, minimal medium calculations, omics data analysis, etc).
- Daniel Machado, Comment on the Manuscript

> If the model is provided with no defined default (i.e. reference) state (because it isn't known), that could somehow be communicated/ detectable to memote so that we can skip tests intelligently. On the other hand, the goal of memote is to help models converge towards the optimal representation of their corresponding biology. Skipping tests at will or because at the present state there is insufficient information on the organism in question seems to reduce the 'effectiveness' of memote as a driver of this change. I mean, once we define and assign weights to each test and calculate a final score, models that fail some tests may still be considered high-quality if they pass others.

- My response to that on the manuscript 

> I agree, but my only concern here is that the exchange reactions define the external environment and not the organism (for the particular case of an FBA simulation they simulate the flow of extracellular compounds in a chemostat).  So, by forcing them to be defined in the model, we are essentially saying that an SBML model is by definition a model of the organism AND the environment. I guess that would be perfectly fine as it would cover most use cases, but it should be made very clear that this is the definition that the field has adopted (1 SBML model = 1 organism + 1 environment).
- Daniel Machado, Follow-up comment on the Manuscript

As for `custom_model`, I think this is sort of beyond its original applicability.  But it would be good if the `custom_model` decorator could take arguments that allow one to be explicit about how each of the arguments to the original function should be interpreted in the context of the model, rather than just by a default interpretation of the arguments.  So for example one could use something like:

```python
@custom_model(inputs=('x', 'y'), parameters('a', 'b'))
def some_function(x, y, a, b, c, d, e):
    ...
```

where that would return a `Model` where the `x` and `y` arguments to `some_function` are taken as the inputs, `a`, and `b` taken as parameters, and `c`, `d`, `e` taken as additional arguments whose interpretation is entirely up to the implementation and are otherwise ignored by the framework.

I wanted to summarize how `TRAVIS_*` env. vars. are used in what @jonparrott [linked to][1]. Essentially, they are only used to detect if on Travis (`${TRAVIS} == true`) and to get a list of changed files in the build:

```
if ${TRAVIS_PULL_REQUEST} == false
  git show --pretty=format: --name-only ${TRAVIS_COMMIT}
else
  try
    git diff --name-only ${TRAVIS_COMMIT} ${TRAVIS_BRANCH}
  fallback
    HEAD_COMMIT = git rev-parse HEAD
    git diff --name-only ${HEAD_COMMIT} ${TRAVIS_BRANCH}
  end
end
```

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/2277#issuecomment-245750250

For GAPIC/gRPC requests, the `google.gax.CallOptions` class does expose a `timeout` field:  we could likely work out how to inject a user-supplied timeout value into the instance which we pass through to the GAPIC API object.

Assuming by "bugs" you mean status codes, by default we retry only on [`UNAVAILABLE` and `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED`](https://github.com/googleapis/toolkit/blob/6bcfcf03d22c549d782f528a13df505a4a6e03e5/src/main/java/com/google/api/codegen/configgen/RetryGenerator.java#L52), and only for idempotent calls.

However, that configuration can be overridden at a per-service level ([example: Pubsub](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/e8de333d3915764dc0cb88170285f01dea4de3a7/google/pubsub/v1/pubsub_gapic.yaml#L53)).

Well, I guess you confuse the two things:

* A short-lived token named Access Token (AT), which is used to access the real resource you want. Since it is short-lived, you will need to somehow "refresh" it periodically.
* A long-lived token named Refresh Token (RT), which is only used for obtaining a new access token.

Those are concepts defined in OAuth2.

Since you are using `acquire_token_with_client_credentials()` to get an access token to representing your app, it will give you an access token (AT) good for typically one hour, WITHOUT a long lived refresh token (RT). And that behavior is by definition/by design, per [OAuth2 spec on Client Credential Grant](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.4.3). So, you are supposed to somehow persist your app's client_id and credential, and call `acquire_token_with_client_credentials()` again to get a new AT, whenever needed.

Somebody more knowledgable may chime in here... But AFAIK I think you're trying to do something PEX hasn't intended to do - make a binary that targets multiple python runtimes. PEX files are designed to be run by a specific Python as demonstrated by the hashbang interpreter header added to the pex file itself. As with virtualenv you are making an environment *with* a specific Python version and it is *for* that python version.

You could in theory make a PEX runnable by multiple python versions if its dependencies were pure python with no version requirements - but this is never true of binary dependencies. Per https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/stable.html the ABI changes for every release of python so I'm guessing it sees having both _cp34_ and _cp35_ distributions as incompatible.

I'm just rambling, but this seems almost feasible.

So here's why I prefer the existing approach:

We can register options (recursive or not) at the global level. We can register at the task level. And then there's this intermediate goal level where you can't actually do anything useful. It seems like a weird discontinuity in functionality. Endowing the goal level with a bit more meat actually seems like a desirable thing to me.

Subsystem feels like the wrong abstraction to me. Those were supposed to be for configuring entities that have a meaningful definition outside of any specific task. Whereas these really are task options, we just want a convenient way of grouping them so we can turn them on and off together.

So using a subsystem feels a bit like treating the problem like a nail because we have this subsystem hammer.  Not to mention the fact that I remain leery of letting subsystems have the same scope as a goal. I'm very unclear on the unintended consequences...

Ha! @fdr , you're a genius. It shouldn't be a surprise but it turns out the issue had nothing to do with wal-e after all. My full restore_command was as follows:

```bash
restore_command =  '. /etc/bashrc ; AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2 wal-e --terse --s3-prefix=s3://mybucket --aws-instance-profile wal-fetch "%f" "%p" 2>&1 | egrep -v "not a lzop file"'
```

What I hadn't realised was that egrep returns a non-zero status if wal-e doesn't produce the string "not a lzop file" ... which it won't do when --terse is turned on, obviously.

The solution was a slightly less brain-dead restore_command:
```bash
  restore_command = '. /etc/bashrc ; o="`AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2 wal-e --terse --s3-prefix=s3://mybucket --aws-instance-profile wal-fetch "%f" "%p" 2>&1`" ; r=$? ; echo "$o" | egrep -v "not a lzop file" ; exit $r'
```

Thanks for pointing me in the right direction, much appreciated.

I find it hard to believe the stemmer versions for MSDOS code pages are any use at all in Java.  I'm dubious they're useful anywhere at this point in time, but in particular Java uses wide character Unicode.

Also, this file really needs to be generated.  Any static file duplicating the list of current stemmers is a maintenance burden when adding new stemmers, and likely to get forgotten sometimes.

I'm not keen for this file to include the rules to run snowball to generate the java stemmer sources - that just duplicates knowledge from `GNUmakefile` and again adds a maintenance burden and opportunities for the two files to get out of step.  I suggest that this file should just go in the tarball created by `make dist_libstemmer_java`, or at least run that command instead of trying to run `snowball` directly.

After looking at L64 and peering at the tests for a bit, I don't understand why you would want to use the fs path as a url at all, when it seems like you always add a url attribute to the dictionary in question anyway: https://github.com/rtibbles/kolibri/blob/learn/kolibri/utils/webpack.py#L119

Note the LOC just above 119 also uses non-Windows friendly paths, although Django seems to handle it ok.

I think there are two possibilities:
1. Don't use the fs path at all to build urls in template tags. This is preferable, unless you have some edge case you're thinking of? But if I modify `kolibri_tags.render_as_url` to *just* use the url attribute instead of the publicPath, everything *seems* to work OK (tests pass, no obvious errors in the site).
2. Account for this previously unanticipated edge case. Means that `kolibri_tags.render_as_url` needs to anticipate being handed a non-Unix path.

For (2), I wrote a test case that looks something like this, which fails on my Windows machines but I expect will pass on Unix machines. In practice we will want test different hard-coded paths (not `__file__` so that the tests are not OS-dependent:

```py
    @override_settings(STATIC_URL=test_url)
    def test_render_as_url_public_path_generates_valid_url(self):
        chunk = {
            "publicPath": os.path.abspath(__file__)  # This should be a legitimate file path.
        }
        val = URLValidator()
        val(kolibri_tags.render_as_url(chunk))  # Will raise an error for invalid urls
```

I think it would be better if we shift this dechuncking practice to the indexer and store actual payload in the IPFS. This will make the replay faster (and less memory intensive). Also, the indexing happens only once while the replay of the same content happens more often, so this can lead to a better optimization. Another advantage of dechuncking at indexing time is the reliable content addressibility. The server might split a response in chunks differently each time the same resource is requested, which would lead to poor deduplication.

For context, this came up with crossbar: if you have a "really small" call_later (e.g. that times out within 200ms) for auto-pings then the autopings "won't fail".

Since these timeouts are supposed to be milliseconds in crossbar's config, it can happen that you e.g. put "60" when you really meant "60000", but you won't notice because you'll hit this bug and Twisted won't do the callback because the `callLater` was a negative timeout...so we should do a new release with this so we can bump the version for the next crossbar release, too.

So, overall I think this runs in the right direction. Let me understand some of the bigger things:

> ApplicationSession a subclass of Session -- and it can then be separate deprecated and removed, ultimately, if desired.

So the idea is, use the new `Session` class to get the new shiny, PEP8 API in place of `ApplicationSession`. But if you have existing code, that continues to run, since the latter is a subclass of the former, and has an implementation that forwards to the new impl. in the base class?

That's true. But, in some cases, one can need a separate handle on vocabulary construction and which can later be used for training purpose. Eg. lets say I want to create a vocabulary out of three different dataset readers / datasets or even 3-4 different experiment configs and then use this collective vocabulary everywhere. This can be done by `make-vocab` pretty easily. Eg. from the comment [here](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/commit/0312b16e23263d24401d7d89b8c327fa0b7259c3#commitcomment-29730621).

Even if `dry-run` does everything that `make-vocab` does, `make-vocab` can come pretty handy for composing vocabularies without doing extra steps (indexing, statistics printing, model loading) that `dry-run` would do. Role of `make-vocab` won't be to catch errors, but to pre construct vocabulary if someone needs.

Role of `dry-run` could be to check that everything is working without errors and configs are appropriate, without actually `train` command. Since now, even model loading is being done, errors in that also would be pre-caught.

This is expected. Within the `Elmo` module, you need a trainable scalar weight matrix that produces a mixtures of the different elmo layers. an operation with a `requires_grad=False` tensor and a `requires_grad=True` tensor (the mixture weights) produces the resultant `requires_grad=True` tensor that you see.

The elmo `requires_grad` construction param should actually be called `trainable` --- it affects whether the _LSTM_ parameters within the `Elmo` module get updated during training or not. By passing `requires_grad=False` into the constructor, we can verify that the LSTM parameters are frozen with:

```
from allennlp.modules.elmo import Elmo, batch_to_ids
options_file = "https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/allennlp/models/elmo/2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway/elmo_2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway_options.json"
weight_file = "https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/allennlp/models/elmo/2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway/elmo_2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway_weights.hdf5"

elmo = Elmo(options_file, weight_file, 1, dropout=0, requires_grad=False)
all(x.requires_grad == False for x in elmo._elmo_lstm.parameters())
>>>False
```

Hope that answers your question.
