bumping this thread now with v2-related discussion. To bring us all up to speed:

 - Deis (now called Workflow) uses Kubernetes instead of Fleet
 - kubernetes has service discovery built into the system, so etcd has been "removed" from the control plane (kubernetes still uses etcd for skydns, it's built-in service discovery mechanism)

The last thing to resolve would be true network segregation based on which namespace the application is deployed. The application should be able to communicate with any other apps deployed in the same namespace to facilitate #4173, but not with the kubernetes API server, the Workflow API server, or any other application outside of its own namespace (internally, at least). Applications should still be able to go surf the public web, as well as target applications through the router. For example, application `foo` can talk to application `bar` via http://bar.example.com (through the router), but not through the pod's IP address (10.247.144.126) unless `bar` happened to be deployed in the same namespace as `foo`.
Deis web is deprecated and unmaintained, we are working on a unofficial ui at [jumbojett/deis-ui](https://github.com/jumbojett/deis-ui).


## Conclusion
- There are no tests for autocorrelation atm, maybe one should add some
- Tbh, I expected the values for autocorr and acf to be the same. What am I missing?
- I tried to match the autocorr results with the acf results, but I failed.
- ACF and PACF generate values outside the value range [-1, 1]. One could catch this as exceptions
- autocorr is using np.corrcoef internally.
- The values of autocorr, acf and pacf are different except for lag 1 but are correlated in general
- Shall PACF be included in tsfresh, too?

## PACF Issue

@LuZhangstat  Please show us the output of `conda info --all`.

I didn't get at first, what was requested, but essentially it's the following difference:
```bash
$ (. etc/profile.d/conda.sh && conda activate base && conda --version)
conda 4.4.9
$ (. etc/profile.d/conda.sh && conda activate env && conda --version)
conda 4.4.9
$ (. etc/profile.d/conda.sh && conda activate base && bash -c 'conda --version')
conda 4.4.9
$ (. etc/profile.d/conda.sh && conda activate env && bash -c 'conda --version')
bash: conda: command not found
```
In the last case the `base` environment (and thus `conda`) isn't on `PATH` (which is a nice `4.4` feature, IMHO!) and we're not trying to run `conda` in a `bash` instance that has the `conda` shell function available; i.e. that `bash -c` represents one arbitrary program that tries to run `conda`.

To me it would make sense to have a `$PREFIX/bin/conda` script in each environment which would `exec` the `base` `conda`. Those scripts could be made into a package, e.g., called something like `conda-launcher`/`conda-scripts`/???. The problem with that would be that, as of know, non-`base` environment have (on their own) no knowledge of the `base` prefix. So you'd either have to use that `CONDA_PYTHON_EXE` workaround with something like:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
exec "$CONDA_PYTHON_EXE" -m conda "${@}"
```
or hardcode the base prefix at install time. As for the former, I don't think there are guarantees that `CONDA_PYTHON_EXE` must be available (and I would rather have `conda` be called instead of that "Python implementation detail"). For the latter, I don't think the base prefix is exposed to post-link scripts or the like.
A third possibility would to always put some directory from `base` on `PATH` that contains some `conda` even if a non-`base` environment is activated. Preferably (to retain that `4.4` feature) this wouldn't be `$CONDA_ROOT/bin` so that only `conda` but nothing else pollutes `PATH`.
In any case (and if I'm not mistaken -- which could very well be) I think a change `conda` would be needed for this feature.

Conda absolutely can tell the owner for each file except in the case of ambiguity in which case just talking the first is fine.  Search for which_package in conda-build (look like my fork for a much faster version).
This issue should be fixed by https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1709 and ready to close.

I'm sorry you're having difficulty, and it is certainly likely there are some remaining bugs to squash and features to implement. But I can assure you that channel priority was designed precisely to address some serious issues that users were having caused by the existence of identical packages in different channels. These problems are real, and they will only get worse, not better, as projects like `conda-forge` or the Intel Python project seek to provide alternative implementations of many core Python packages. 

My suggestion to you is to go ahead and use `channel_priority: false`, since that closely mirrors the behavior you are accustomed to. It's not *quite* the same, however, because even with `channel_priority: false`, channel priority takes precedence over build number---because build numbers _cannot_ be compared reliably across channels. Still, at least with `channel_priority: false`, you can be sure to the get the latest _version_ of a package no matter what.

For `--use-index-cache` it just uses the local index, and that index might well contain packages that are not available locally and in that case conda would still go online and fetch them.

`--offline` on the other hand makes conda not consider packages from non-local channels. Even if they are already downloaded. I think it implies `--use-index-cache`.

What I'm after is basically something that sees (at least) all already-downloaded packages and can install them when I have no connectivity. In 4.2.x `--use-index-cache` was doing that.

Maybe `--use-index-cache` was rather meant as an optimization for the case where one runs multiple conda commands in succession and wants to speed up those operations by not retrieving the index of every channel for every single command. In that case I guess the HTTP caching logic that's in place now should take care of that already.

So a more forward-looking solution could be to remove `--use-index-cache` and instead make `--offline` work with both local channels and packages from non-local channels that are available locally? In that case one detail to think about would be what to do if there are two packages available satisfying the constraints and one is available locally but the solver prefers the other one (e.g. a more recent package compared to the local one is available online).

Thanks for pinging me on this important discussion. I think the most important task at the moment is to specify formally the rules how feature resolution is supposed to behave, such that there is a unique definition of what's going to happen in every possible situation. This should include if and when featured packages shall be selected, but also when error messages like 'unsatisfiable installation request' or 'ambiguous installation request' occur in the presence of features and which hints are then desirable. Currently, we only have a bunch of poorly documented heuristics, and that's not good enough.

IMHO, @mcg1969's approach to treat features like dependencies is correct. In fact, that's what I do explicitly when I work around the current limitations by means of version tags. That is, I create version numbers like `foo-1.2.3.vc14` to indicate that the package 'foo' defines `track_features: - vc14` or `features: - vc14`. Consequently, the normal version resolution algorithm can be used to enforce a feature as a dependency. Specifically, dependents of 'foo' will simply write
```
requirements:
  run:
    - foo  >=1.2,*.vc14*
```
The improved feature resolution algorithm should behave semantically like this, but should add the `,*.vc14*` part automatically where appropriate. (See also https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1959 for a proposal how explicit version tags can be added automatically using jinja2 templates.)

Regarding @pelson's example above: After `conda remove my_meta_package`, there are two admissible policies for the subsequent `conda update --all` (neithor of which is currently realized): It could either replace package 'a' with its featureless variant 'v2', or it could keep the installed 'v1', but repair it by re-installing its dependency 'my_meta_package'. The rules mentioned above should exactly specify what's going to happen. Out of my head, I'd prefer the second resolution.

It is also important that the rules distinguish two fundamentally different cases:
* Packages that never define a certain feature. For example, a pure Python package doesn't typically define a compiler feature.
* Packages where a particular feature is only missing in certain versions, but defined in others. For example, numpy 1.10 might be available in 'mkl' and 'openblas' variants, but when upgrading to numpy 1.11, only the 'mkl' variant is supplied (at least temporarily). 

In the second case, the rules should have the effect that version 1.11 is excluded from all version resolution as long as `track_features: - openblas` is active in the current environment. This is currently not happening, see https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589.

I suggest that the conda designers come up with a draft for the rules how features should behave that we can then discuss.

```vhdl
entity tb is
end entity;
```

Thanks for the issue @melissachang, and I'm sorry to hear you're having some troubles. 

This is something other people have come across as well (please see https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/issues/715). But building this into the client is not something I feel would add any value. If someone configures their ES instances incorrectly, we'll end up swallowing errors and nothing will get reported. (even if we built a timeout into it, we have to wait till the timeout expires before the application reports that the cluster is no reachable). 

I feel that this is something that should be solved in the application code that implements the Elasticsearch client and not something that should be built into the client itself. 

The standard pattern for connecting to any service in docker is to build a "wait" into the application code that waits for a service to start before continuing.  Please reference the Docker documentation on [controlling startup order](https://docs.docker.com/compose/startup-order/).

I have in the past created this bash script which will wait for elasticsearch before starting the command in your container: 

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

host="$1"
shift
cmd="$@"


until $(curl --output /dev/null --silent --head --fail "$host"); do
    printf '.'
    sleep 1
done

# First wait for ES to start...
response=$(curl $host)

until [ "$response" = "200" ]; do
    response=$(curl --write-out %{http_code} --silent --output /dev/null "$host")
    >&2 echo "Elastic Search is unavailable - sleeping"
    sleep 1
done


# next wait for ES status to turn to Green
health="$(curl -fsSL "$host/_cat/health?h=status")"
health="$(echo "$health" | sed -r 's/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$//g')" # trim whitespace (otherwise we'll have "green ")

until [ "$health" = 'green' ]; do
    health="$(curl -fsSL "$host/_cat/health?h=status")"
    health="$(echo "$health" | sed -r 's/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$//g')" # trim whitespace (otherwise we'll have "green ")
    >&2 echo "Elastic Search is unavailable - sleeping"
    sleep 1
done

>&2 echo "Elastic Search is up"
exec $cmd
```

You can use this script in your `Dockerfile` with:
```dockerfile
CMD ["/code/wait-for-elasticsearch.sh", "http://elasticsearch:9200", "--", "binary", "command", "sub-command"]
```

or similarly you can use this script in your `docker-compose.yml` by overriding the `command` in a similar fashion.

Hi @annisele the reason you get that error is because you are providing a resource by name and thus you have to provide the other parameters, at a minimum `--resource-group` and `--resource-type`.  If you provide only `--resource` then you must supply a full resource ID, not just a name. That's what that usage statement is telling you.

There is a bug though, since it appears as "resource_uri-X" in the message instead of "resource-X". We will get that fixed in an upcoming release.

1. Good point. It probably doesn't need to be a class. `ReplayableTest` stores an instance of `TestConfig` as an attribute but we really only care about `TestConfig.args`. It can probably just be a function.
2. I agree it is weird. I use [ConfigArgParse](https://github.com/bw2/ConfigArgParse) just to get the config file and env var handling, which has exactly the order of precedence that we want (environment variables > config file values > defaults). But it's based on argparse, and specifying commandline parameters to tests is unorthodox at best (plus it's not clear that consumers of azure-devtools will have a consistent way of doing so), so I skip the argument parsing part of it entirely. Nevertheless, it seems to be the easiest off-the-shelf way to get the configuration mechanism we want.

Hi, I'm already used to add labels to the issues/PRs I read (and understand) in all of projects I maintain. As for me, it's just a good common sense of maintaining OSS.

- first of all, I _support_ the need to have all label meanings documented (sometimes it's not easy for me to understand meanings of some of them)
- second, it's important to focus on newbie-friendly labels better: github suggests having label called `good first issue`. Names like this tell them more than just `easy`, we probably need to focus on making existing labels better understandable outside of the committers circle
- also, I'd encourage everyone to pay attention on world-promoted activities like `Hacktoberfest` (although this year's one is over now). **I've tested this one in several places and a few of marked issues _got solved almost immediately_.**

I'm not sure I understand the idea of having a priority queue. The way that I've been thinking about it is:

* With the exception of switching into edit mode, and marking/unmarking cells, all other actions are only ever applied to marked cells. The state of being marked is indicated by a bar to the left of the cell.
* In order to choose a cell that you want to edit, or to mark/unmark individual cells, you have a cursor. If the cursor is on a cell, then that cell is counted as marked and thus has a bar to the left of it as well.
* However, when the cursor is on a cell, while that cell is counted as marked, it is not a "sticky" mark, e.g. it will go away if you focus on another cell. So you can explicitly mark the cell, but then you need a way of visually distinguishing cells that are focused that have do or do not have a sticky mark.

So, essentially, it should be possible to determine from a screenshot (without knowing the history of actions) which of the following categories each cell falls under:

* Focused/selected (with a "sticky" mark)
* Focused/selected (without a "sticky" mark)
* Not selected and marked
* Not selected and unmarked

> By forcing the logs to be structured, with {name} formatting and

means move from:
```
log("Lorem ipsum '%s' dolor sit amet=%d, consectetur" % (a, b))
```

to

```
log("Lorem ipsum '{ssn}' dolor sit amet={duration}, consectetur", ssn=ssn, duration=duration)
```

In the first case the log method get the formatted message – which is hard to extract. We could use positional formatting, but then we loose information as to which fields are sensitive: `(45612789, 12)` is not helpful to filter, `{ ssn:45612789, duration:12}`

The final revision of this patch adds a `convert=True/False` switch to `field.validate()`, `Model.validate()`, and the main `validate()` functions. It's now possible to skip the `to_native` step that is normally bundled with validation.

This allows us to move the conversion task inside `field.validate()` without any loss of flexibility. As explained earlier, moving the `to_native()` call is the only correct way to "fix" #338.

The `convert` option turns out to be useful way beyond the original purpose of this PR. Consider:

```python
m = SomeModel(<raw data>) # primitive-to-native conversion
m.validate()              # implies unnecessary native-to-native conversion
```

Now you can say:

```python
m.validate(convert=False)
```

when the datatypes are known to be good and only need to be validated. This is great for performance when only trusted operations have been performed after the initial import.

There were at least two validators that essentially duplicated the functionality of `to_native`:
  * `NumberType.validate_is_a_number()`
  * `ObjectIdType.validate_id()`.

These methods are removed.

This is difficult. One use case for grow-cluster is to grow a cluster to a previous size, for example after all spot workers were wiped out during a price increase. One would expect the replacement nodes to attach to the existing volumes as opposed to creating new volumes. To support this naively, we'd have to ignore `--ebs-volume-size` for replacement nodes and only apply it to new nodes and hence new volumes. The more advanced way of solving this would be to "resize" the volumes before attaching them. That would be pretty involved. You'd have to copy the volume and grow the file system inside it. You could only grow, not shrink.

Great discussion.  🙂  Efficiency is the primary reason for the creation and use of the GeneratorContainers (that incrementally yield results only as needed/requested by your code).

A Python generator is a great tool for representing the "lists" of items that can be returned by Cisco Spark as they help with the following challenges and more:

 * Let's say you are looking for a particular room (find by title).  When requesting the list of rooms, you don't know beforehand how many rooms might be needed to find the one that you are looking for, and requesting "all" of the rooms is wasteful (mostly a waste of time).  Compare the response time for a list of 10 items to a list of 1,000 items; if what you are looking for can be found in the first 10 rooms (or 20) requesting small incremental pages will speed up the responsiveness of your script.

* What about when you want to look at the messages in a room?  How far back do you want to go?  Messages from today, since yesterday, this week, this month?  How many messages might be represented in those time increments?  Trying to request a static number of messages would be a guessing game and error prone (and you would have to deal with paging).

* What if you really do want **all** of the items (all of the rooms, all of the messages, and etc.)?  How many items would you have to request?  If it is larger than Spark's default page size maximum, you would have to handle paging.  How many pages would it be?

Generator functions provide efficient and flexible functionality to suit a wide variety of use cases:

 * If you want to iterate through a "list" of items from the cloud and stop when you have found what you are looking for, you can do that (only the minimally needed pages of items are requested).
``` python
for room in api.rooms.list():
    if room.title == "The room I am looking for...":
        found_it = room
        break
# The ciscosparkapi.room.list generator will request a page of items, yielding them one at a time
# to your code (your for loop) requesting additional pages as needed until you either break out of
# the loop (you found what you were looking for) or all items have been returned (at which point
# the for look will exit.
```

 * If you want some specific number of items...  You can do that efficiently.
``` python
from itertools import islice
from ciscosparkapi import CiscoSparkAPI

NUMBER_OF_MESSAGES = 2000

api = CiscoSparkAPI()
messages = api.messages.list(roomId='<your-room-id>', max=NUMBER_OF_MESSAGES)

short_list = list(islice(messages, NUMBER_OF_MESSAGES))

# By setting the maximum number of items per page to the total number that you want, if Spark
# will allow sending that many items in a single response - cool you'll get them all in one response. 
# If not, the ciscosparkapi package will transparently handle the requesting of additional pages for
# you, and the Python standard library `islice()` iterator-slicer will ensure that only the requested
# number of items will be yielded and passed to the `list()` function - result you will have a list
# containing up to `NUMBER_OF_MESSAGES` messages.
```

 * If you really want **all** of the items in a real Python list?  Also, easy:
``` python
room_list = list(api.rooms.list())
```

So, no matter how many items you want returned:  a few, some in-determinant number of items, or if you want all of the items - a generator function is the perfect single interface to accommodate all of these needs as efficiently as the APIs and your use case will allow.  If you need all the items, we'll really have to get them all.  If you need some, we'll stop when you tell us.  😎

So, that's what generators do!  ...but what's up with the "containers?"  Well, the short answer is that an iterator returned by a generator function is a single-use sort of thing.  Once you have iterated it, you can't iterate it again. You would have to call the generator function to get another iterator.  This is a pain and error prone, so I "wrapped" the generator functions in a container that makes them safe for reuse.  Every time you iterate one of our generator containers, the container takes care of creating a "fresh" iterator for you.

> Since `MULTI_OPTION` attribute type is merged, we can think about possibility of switching from product option to attribute option.

No, I disagree. *Attributes* and *options* are two different things and they need to be kept separate. 

*Attributes* are simply structured product information - a way to store information about products in a way that allows you to search/filter based on them. For example, a laptop might have a `disk_space` attribute where you can store how big the hard disk is. This allows you to search/filter based on that as well as display this information in a structured way to the customer.

*Options* are specific purchase choices that are selected when a customer makes a purchase. e.g., they might be able to select a certain image to be printed on their T-shirt, or select a specific colour for their laptop.

The `MULTI_OPTION` *attribute* doesn't help here - it is still an attribute, not a purchase option. For example, a laptop could have a `output_type` attribute that allows multiple values (multi option) like `HDMI`, and `VGA`. A laptop can support both. These are just features of the laptop, not options for purchase.

There are some cases where options and attributes look very similar (e.g., the colour of a T-shirt) but these are special cases. For that reason we need to keep them separate. Maybe we can rename `Option` if it is not clear how it is different from `AttributeOption`.

`--print-key-file` doesn't sound like a useful plugin-like integration. Users see the certificate path once its issued in `letsencrypt auth` command and they can just copy-paste it. Let's Encrypt plugins are a bit more complicated conceptually...

Authenticator plugins are meant to perform challenges, which requires interaction with the webserver configuration (in order to have no downtimes). This doesn't have to involve config file updates - you could provide API on the webserver side to update configuration "on the fly" (in memory), e.g. `h2octl add-new-letsencrypt-dvsni-challenge-host --key foo --cert bar ...` and then create a python plugin that exploits this API. Otherwise, people have to stop the webserver (at least for port 443), use `letsencrypt auth` and afterwards set the paths to the issued certificates manually,

Installer plugins (`letsencrypt install`) are passed certificate paths and it's up to them to decide how to interact with the webserver. You could for example put certificates and keys into predefined directory structure, say `/etc/h20/letsencrypt/$domain/{key,cert}.pem`, and code the server, so that it defaults to reading those files if no key/cert for SSL was provided. This wouldn't require any interaction with the config files.

As you can see, there are other ways than changing the configuration files directly. Use your imagination! :)

In 1), 2), and the status quo, changes to the crypto libraries we use can result in causing parsing of either new or old keys to break (depending on the chosen scenario).

The highest level solution to this problem is dependency pinning (which we use already) and good testing. If there were a test that generated new keys and then used them, then when the dependency pinnings were updated, the tests would have failed, and that update would never have been merged into `develop`, and we wouldn't be here.

Even if we did 2) (which seems to me, at first, the more attractive solution), it can still cause keys we use to break when the libraries are updated. And code will still break if we write code to parse all key types and there

In all cases, we need testing for the key formats we support, so we may as well start with that, I suppose. Beyond that, I prefer 2) if it's readily feasible, and if it's not readily feasible, then either 1) or, I suppose, neither (and make it explicit somewhere what PEM formats we support, and add support for the new one).

```
while True:

       self._client.produce(..)
       break
     except BufferError:
        # Try again in 100ms or sooner if a delivery report is triggered
        self.poll(0.1)
        continue
```

1) +1: good to know!

2) All callbacks require some means of triggering them, which is either by `poll()` or `flush()` (and to some extent `consumer.close()`).
While librdkafka could spontaneously trigger these callbacks from its internal threads it would put the burden on thread semantics and locking on the application developer, something they might not be familiar with. So to save them from thelseves this choice was made that all callbacks are called in an application thread, and it is up to the application developer to decide when and where.
So I think your best choice is either a separate python thread that calls poll() (it will then also serve the delivery callbacks), or that you stick `poll(0)` calls here and there in-between other blocking calls.

The `log_cb` is currently not exposed to Python.

Callbacks, whatever they are: error_cb, delivery reports, etc, are queued internally in librdkafka until the application calls poll() (or similar interface that calls poll() underneath, such as flush()).
So there is no memory leak per say because the objects are still tracked, and if the producer or consumer instance is destroyed prior to serving all those callbacks with poll() they will be freed, thus not resulting in a memory leak.

I see that the library you have referenced already implements a pySerial (<3.0) compatible class (It would require minor changes, renamed methods names etc. to update for 3.0).

An URL handler is identified by the protocol (the `xxx` part in `xxx://...`) and a module with the name `protocol_xxx` is imported (searched in the registered packages in `serial.protocol_handler_packages`. the `protocol_xxx` module itself just implements a `Serial` class will all the functions required by pySerial. Alternatively the module `protocol_xxx` can also provide a function `serial_class_for_url(url)` that returns a `Serial` compatible object.

See `serial/urlhandler/protocol_socket.py` for a simple example of an URL handler.

for the `ft232`library it would basically be a file `protocol_ftdi.py` with the contents `from libftdi import LibFtdi as Serial` (for pySerial <3.0 but for the version here on github, the ft232 would need an update to the latest pySerial API) and it may need a tweak to handle the URL that is assigned to its `.port` attribute.

```
pip install mxnet-cu80==1.1.0
```

https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/master/LinuxGPU.md

These instructions assume that you are on Linux, since that's the OS of most of our CUDA users. The code below should allow you to monkey patch your modelchain objects until we get this fixed:

```python
import types
def effective_irradiance_model_corrected(self):
    fd = self.system.module_parameters.get('FD', 1.)
    self.effective_irradiance = self.spectral_modifier * (
        self.total_irrad['poa_direct']*self.aoi_modifier.fillna(0) +
        fd*self.total_irrad['poa_diffuse'])
    return self

mc = ModelChain(system, location, **your_normal_kwargs)
mc.effective_irradiance_model = types.MethodType(effective_irradiance_model_corrected, mc)
mc.run_model(times, weather=weather)  # as if nothing has changed
```

It looks like the problem may be caused by [this line](https://github.com/IDSIA/sacred/blob/576803abd2fa4d5945ceedbd0bf5a7db6daac0d8/sacred/initialize.py#L124). If `get_config_updates_recursive` returns an empty dict, this means that there are no config updates. This dict is then embedded into the config updates of the surrounding ingredient and through this becomes an empty dict inside of a configuration, which will not be removed due to the fix of #262.

Maybe the code could be modified like this:
```python
def get_config_updates_recursive(self):
        config_updates = self.config_updates.copy()
        for sr_path, subrunner in self.subrunners.items():
            update = subrunner.get_config_updates_recursive()
            if len(update) > 0:
                config_updates[sr_path] = update
        return config_updates
```

This would prevent empty config updates to be added to surrounding configs. This modification fixes the bug in my case. Though, I'm not sure if this raises any further issues.

@wintersteiger Looking at this, I don't understand why so many different paths are being searched for `libz3`. This will lead to all sorts of horrible issues when trying to use z3 from the build directory when another copy of z3 is also installed.

We **know** the `libz3` binary we want to use can only be in one of two possible locations

* In the build tree, next to the `z3` python package directory.
* In the install tree. We can compute this path at run time relative to the python package directory so that the install is portable.

We shouldn't really be considering **any other paths**. 

* Checking `PATH` is **wrong**. This is for binaries, not libraries.
* Checking `Z3_LIBRARY_PATH` is probably okay as this is a custom Z3 specific environment variable but I would still heavily discourage this because it can easily lead to some shooting themselves in the foot. I see no reason to support this environment variable.
* Checking `builtins.Z3_LIB_DIRS`... is this even legal?? I don't see why anyone would ever need this.
* Checking the current working directory seems pretty nasty and super fragile. This caused me to miss the fact that the z3 python package in the build tree **ONLY works if the current working directory is the `python` directory in the build tree**.

Thank you! The choice is actually arbitrary, there's nothing preventing you from using the last dimension for depth and it shouldn't make a difference for the implementation either as long all spatial dimensions are grouped together. > There are places where input types are being checked using isinstance(val, int), which fails if val is a type np.int64

Only in Python 3:
```bash
python3
>>> import numpy as np
>>> isinstance(np.int64(123), int)
False
```

Not in Python 2:
```bash
python2
>>> import numpy as np
>>> isinstance(np.int64(123), int)
True
```

And your proposed solution does not fix this either:
```bash
python3
>>> from numbers import Integral
>>> isinstance(123, Integral)
True
>>> import numpy as np
>>> isinstance(np.int64(123), Integral)
False
```

Do you see a different behaviour on your machine?

Your proposal would help with the `long` type in Python 2, though. When using Python 2 under Windows, ndarray shapes are `long` instead of `int`, which won't pass an `isinstance(..., int)` check. And if we want to explicitly allow numpy integers, we could use `isinstance(..., (numbers.Integral, np.integer))`.

So in general I'm all for avoiding cryptic/unfair errors. We'll need to check in detail whether this should be done by a relaxed type check or an explicit `int` conversion. Would you be willing to work on this? What was your use case / how did you find out about this?

I just realized we can achieve the 2nd point mentioned in my issue quite easily. The 2nd point was to return the parameters of only the branches (layers between end of a branch and last shared layer). This can be done by using one of the get_all_layers() parameters, **treat_as_input**. 

So it'd be something like this,
```
def get_all_params(layer, unwrap_shared=True, treat_as_input=None, **tags):

    layers = get_all_layers(layer,treat_as_input)
    params = chain.from_iterable(l.get_params(unwrap_shared=unwrap_shared, **tags) for l in layers)
    return utils.unique(params)
```

This behaviour is certainly not wanted but sadly there is no easy universal fix.

The way the player works at the moment is that it  Downloads the first file you requested, opens the player which plays the song and shuts down. MPSYT waits for the player to exit before it picks the next song in your playlist and repeats the process and because we don't know the audio level when the player shuts down we can't initiate the next player with the same audio level.

Until we fix this somehow; If you are using `mpv` as your music player of choice, you can use `set playerargs` to specifically set the volume with a `set playerargs --volume=<0-100>` 

Or if you want to just loop one song over and over again `set playerargs --loop=<n or inf>` where `n` is the number of times you want to play the song, or `inf` if you want it to loop forever. Because we only supply the player with one song at the time, this will loop only the first song, even if you select more than one from your search results.

Hope that can be an adequate "hack" for the time being!

@computron, perhaps a first issue to address is "what is our intended use for the `PropertyStats.std_dev` function?" 

There seem to be two possible cases for employing property stats. A first one is measuring the variance in some property across a dataset. In that case, I agree that using the Bessel correction is appropriate because we are estimating the variance with only a sample of all possible entries. 

The other case is using the function is more like measuring the variance as it relates to a certain entry in the dataset. For example, this could be the variance a property across the elements present in the material or across all sites in a crystal. In those cases, we are measuring the variance across the entire population (e.g., all sites in a crystal) and the uncorrected measurement seems more appropriate.

Given that the only current use of PropertyStats in matminer is for the the latter case, I vote we keep using the uncorrected standard deviation by default. I think we should support both as an option, as that we help ensure users of the PropertyStats functions consider which is most appropriate for their application. 

Sound reasonable?

We need to decide on how we want multifeaturizer to work, we have three options currently proposed

(1) - What's in this pull requests changes `MultiFeaturizer.featurize_dataframe` to run the featurizers sequentially, rather than in aggregate using the `MultiFeaturizer.featurize` method and the inherited `BaseFeaturizer.featurizer_dataframe`.  FunctionFeaturizer is compatible with MultiFeaturizer (and presumably any Featurizer that overrides featurize_dataframe is also), but we are left with potential issues with caching.
(2) - I can revert the changes so we keep what's been changed in FunctionFeaturizer (which I think is an improvement, even if #217 isn't addressed).  FunctionFeaturizer will not be compatible with MultiFeaturizer.
(3) - We could adopt a default mode for multifeaturizer if none of the included featurizers override featurize_dataframe which would do everything in parallel, and then a sequential mode if any of the included featurizers override featurize_dataframe, issuing a warning that performance may be hindered by the sequential operation.

The global **can_clip** variable exists as a global boolean flag to reflect whether or not the ``paperclip`` module has loaded correctly and we thus have proper access to clipboard/pastebuffer cut-and-paste functionality.  It is only used in two places:

1. cmd2.py:  cmd2.Cmd._redirect_output()
2. test_cmd2.py:  test_send_to_paste_buffer()

I don't see any reason why it could become a boolean instance variable within cmd2.Cmd.  If we could 100% trust ``paperclip`` to always work all the time on all versions of that module, Python, and OSes then we wouldn't need this variable at all.  But in the past we had some issues when new versions of ``paperclip`` come out, so this is intended to help ``cmd2`` remain functional just without that one feature if/when that happens.

Moving those two functions to utils.py should be straightforward.

it's difficult to tell but I'd guess that somewhere you have a separate thread that works with the tag. The traceback above starts at line 286 of mainNfc.py with checking tag.ndef or wifiSettings but wifiSettings is a variable and must be set from somewhere else. If this "somewhere else" piece of code had attempted to read but failed, or had for whatever reason issued a new clf.connect (or clf.sense) call, then this may have caused the subsequent error.

For a bit of background, the ContactlessFrontend implementation memorizes the current target found in the *latest* clf.sense (internally used by clf.connect) call. Any further call to clf.sense replaces that target with a new result - None if no target responds. A Type2Tag may sometimes return a NAK response (negative acknowledge) and then need to be re-sensed. If the Type2Tag would now be gone, the tag object's next attempt to read data fails with the TypeError you've seen. This is now corrected to correctly raise TagCommandError(TIMOUT_ERROR). For code that just works with the NDEF data it should all be handled well - there either is or is not an NdefMessage in the tag.ndef attribute.

/Stephen

> What are your thoughts on creating a distinction between application and view routing?

I think we should only provide routing to ASGI applications. For class based views that works seemlessly, since View *is* an ASGI app.

For function based views, we’ve got a request/response interface tho. Either you can wrap that function up in a decorator to make it an ASGI app (That’s what we currently have documented, tho I don’t like that it changes the signature, makes the interface less directly testable) or instead wrap the adapter around the function within the routing, something like ‘Path(‘/blah’, app=asgi_application(my_view))’

Right now I see two possible options:

1. Only care about a minimal subset of the most common attributes. Order everything else after that in alphabetical order. This will undoubtedly leave some attributes ordered in a non-optimum way but should be easier to implement and is probably more future proof.
2. Try to achieve a useful order for all attributes. This would already be pretty hard for the attributes specified in SVG 1.1 alone, however (if done right) it would be the better solution by far.

If we decide on the first option I'd suggest the following order:
id/class -> transform -> x,y,z,width,height,x1,x2,y1,y2,cx,cy,r,rx,ry,fx,fy ->d ->[properties](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/propidx.html) -> style -> everything else in alphabetic order

If we decide on the second option I'd follow the order in the SVG 1.1 specification (see for example [the path element](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/paths.html#PathElement)) with the exception to put id/class at the start. Like before everything else goes at the end in alphabetical order.

Sure, here's a basic TPOT workflow:

```Python
from tpot import TPOTClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

digits = load_digits()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(digits.data, digits.target,
                                                    train_size=0.75, test_size=0.25)

tpot = TPOTClassifier(generations=100, population_size=100, # TPOT will evaluate 100x100 pipelines
                      verbosity=2, # TPOT will show a progress bar during optimization
                      n_jobs=-1, # Enable multiprocessing
)
tpot.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(tpot.score(X_test, y_test))
```

More examples available in the docs [here](http://epistasislab.github.io/tpot/examples/).

One of the major challenges of making the Relief-based algorithms of ReBATE flexible enough to handle different dataset types, i.e. (1) continuous, discrete, or mixed feature types, (2) binary, multiclass, or continuous outcomes, (3) presence of missing data.  Presently scikit-rebate is implemented in a fairly compact manner, however this may not ultimately be the most efficient implementation.  This issue posting seeks enhancements to ReBATE and it's underlying algorithms (i.e. ReliefF, SURF, SURF*, MultiSURF, MultiSURF*, TuRF) to make the respective algorithms run faster, and utilize less memory.

Coming back to this with a general idea of a workflow and some issues:

Workflow:
1. First pass through dataset, preprocess, build vocabs for fields, store example lengths
2. Fix vocabs according to vocab_size, min_freq for each vocab, perhaps do some bucketing of examples according to their lengths
3. Second pass through dataset, numericalize (but don't pad), pickle dataset in blocks, save vocabs & fields
4. Train time, load blocks from serialized file, pad and convert into batches

Issues:
- Dataset blocks are going to have elements of uneven lengths (as is the standard case with text). Which is the most efficient way of storing lists of (lists? numpy arrays? tensors?)?
    - I'll try out each of these and see what works fastest and takes the least disk size
    - This can be somewhat alleviated by bucketing (1.), but it seems like overkill
- Should the preprocessed dataset be stored? If no, the preprocessing (essentially, splitting) is going to be done twice (in 1. and 2.). I see the argument for yes (faster) and no (more disk space used). This can be an optional argument to the implementation.
- 1 & 3 can be done in one pass without the nice frequency-sorted vocab indices. This could also be an optional argument.

I'll get an alpha PR running in about two weeks time, and I'd appreciate some feedback (esp. with regard to efficient block storage)

Oh, I see. In this case, the code is actually succeeding, so there is no error message.

We may change things so that this fails in the future, but right now the remote function `f` is not being pickled and exported until `ray.init()` is called. By the time that happens, `helper` has already been defined and so everything works.

You can see the error if you instead run

```python
import ray
import time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f(x):
    return helper(x)


def helper(x):
    return x + 1

f.remote(0)

time.sleep(10)
```

Note that the `time.sleep` is there to prevent the script from exiting before it has the chance to print the error message, since the error is printed asynchronously in the background.

Update:
The primary cause is overloading the redis client (local scheduler) pubsub buffer, configured by default to be 32MB size as a hard limit and only an 8MB soft-limit. If the latter persists for more than 60s (which was the case), the redis client is disconnected. Source:
https://github.com/antirez/redis/blob/d680eb6dbdf2d2030cb96edfb089be1e2a775ac1/redis.conf

To get the configured limit:
`CONFIG GET client-output-buffer-limit` inside a redis-client attached to the running redis-server.

We address this by increasing both the hard and soft limits for the redis client pubsub buffer to 128MB:
`CONFIG SET client-output-buffer-limit "normal 0 0 0 slave 268435456 67108864 60 pubsub 134217728 134217728 60"`

The secondary cause is numerous retries triggered due to using a default redis retry data struct with unlimited retries. Over 200 thousand retries were observed at one point when issuing 1M tasks. We will separately investigate disabling retries.

I move the coroutine wrapper into its own module, which exposes a single global instance that can track coroutine creation. And this instance is shared between the `MultiErrors.catch` logic and the `trio.run`.
`Trio.run` (when configured to disallow un-awaited coroutine) will install a `coroutine_wrapper`, and uninstall it on exit, if a `coro_wrapper` is already set it will wrap it, and restore it on exit. 

One thing I'm unsure is whether I should check regularly that we are still the installed hook on set_coro_wrapper, and/or wether we are still regularly called – but we can probably push that on the user: if you are setting a coroutine wrapper, the you are responsible to still call us. 

I can do that, by actually creating a unawaited coroutine and checking that the coro_wrapper have intercepted it, but that might be for later and not obviously necessary. 

The error messages could be better especially when the unawaited coroutine is not followed by a checkpoint before task exit (teach except_hook to skip some frames ?)

No, it just calls `os.listdir` and then yields each element.  The following is what's in the stdlib:

```python
def iterdir(self):
    """Iterate over the files in this directory.  Does not yield any
    result for the special paths '.' and '..'.
    """
    if self._closed:
        self._raise_closed()
    for name in self._accessor.listdir(self):
        if name in {'.', '..'}:
            # Yielding a path object for these makes little sense
            continue
        yield self._make_child_relpath(name)
        if self._closed:
            self._raise_closed()
```

where `self._accessor.listdir` is just `os.listdir` unless you pass something else as the `pathlib.Path` template argument, and `os.listdir` just returns a plain list.

My issue with options like sprout/bough/limb is that those sound like they refer to the children, while we need a name for the point where the children are attached.

@smurfix has a good point about "branch" already being overloaded, and as a control flow thing, so that's definitely confusing.

Of the nursery alternatives... maybe "splitpoint" is the best I can come up with so far? (As in, "this is a point where control can split".) It has some resonance with "checkpoint", which is our [other bit of funny terminology](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#checkpoints):

```python
async with trio.open_splitpoint() as splitpoint:
    splitpoint.start_soon(...)
```

I actually kind of like how this de-emphasizes the concept of "children", since trio doesn't really reify tasks/threads/whatever-you-wanna-call-them. There are no task ids or task objects or anything (unless you go digging into `trio.hazmat`). OTOH we still need to refer to the different concurrent functions in conversation -- I suppose we could call them "splits", like: "While the first split is doing name resolution, the other splits are waiting...". Maybe that's too weird?

("sproutpoint" is also an option, but that might be too silly even for me :-).)

I suppose there's also

```python
async with trio.open_family() as family:
    family.start_soon(...)
```

but I feel like "family" is too commonly used as a kind of generic word for "category". "This family of concurrency frameworks uses families...", ick.

> You dooo mention coroutine ! :-)

*shhhhh*

> open_nursery feel weird, I like your NurseryManager. Or static method on Nursery?

I don't think this is going to change at this point -- after a few months of usage it still seems fine to me, and the name is part of a general convention for functions that open a resource with a bounded lifetime that go in a with block (`trio.open_cancel_scope`, `trio.open_file`, `trio.open_tcp_stream`, ...).

> Could IPython (as interactive REPL) have an AST-checker to check you await all coroutine and help with potential mistakes ?

That's a neat idea. I think it's basically https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9251? You could talk to the author of that bug maybe ;-)

@roycaihw  Many thanks for your response. [kubernetes_asyncio](https://github.com/tomplus/kubernetes_asyncio) is almost identical from user point of view (only changing imports and async syntax are required). I watch the official repository and catch up changes. It will be much easier if the library became experimental/official. Thanks for considering this issue.

Unfortunately there are a lot of changes related to classes from [python-base](https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python-base). To be honest I had to rewrite [Watch](https://github.com/tomplus/kubernetes_asyncio/blob/master/kubernetes_asyncio/watch/watch.py), [Stream](https://github.com/tomplus/kubernetes_asyncio/tree/master/kubernetes_asyncio/stream) to be able to work in the asynchronous environment. We can try using the named branches but it may be confusing for contributors. I have other 2 options to discuss:

- Removing/merging python-base repository - I know it’s a controversial idea and I know the [motivation](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design-proposals/api-machinery/csi-client-structure-proposal.md), but I don’t know another library which uses `python-base` with other API groups. Is it still important for you? BTW only Python and Go use base-repos, other client libraries (Java, JS, C#, Haskell, Ruby) don’t have such base repos. 

- I can create dedicated classes for asyncio with different names (like `AsyncWatch`, `AsyncStream` etc.) in `python-base` repo. This classes will be imported by `kubernetes_asyncio` and exported again with previous name. This trick allows us to have the common `python-base` and consistent libraries.

WRT final libraries, I think it’d be better to have libraries in separate repositories instead of branches, it’s more common to have a repository per package, it’s easy to install from repo, issues are tracked separately etc.

@wagamamaz I thin your remarks comes from a misunderstanding. We are perfectly aware of the case you would like to use, and it works perfectly with the new API.

I said we would like to have a more Keras-like API because it is more intuitive and a cleaner approach. I never said it has to be identical and work the same way.

The few times I try to use Keras to see how it works, I also struggled on similar situations as you pointed out. The plan is **by no mean** to complexify or prevent users to do things that they currently do easily.

Unfortunately, I can't share with you some code. Because, it's still not perfectly working. However, as I said in our previous meeting.

If any important feature (e.g non sequential model) can't be made to work easily and smoothly, **We will cancel this update and abort the update**. This would be **an absolute no-go**. I 100% agree with you.

-------------

I would also like to highlight something, we used the term `Network.compile()`, maybe this was unfortunate and shall be renamed (any proposition ?). TL compile and Keras compile are by no mean identical. 

Keras compile takes a loss and an optimizer. In the other hand, at the current stage, TL compile is only a function that will generate the corresponding TF Ops/Tensors/Vars of each Layer.

In the new API, Network and Layers shall be understood as "blueprints" of networks and layers. The `.compile()` method create the corresponding TF Ops/Tensors/Vars for each Layer/Network.

I hope to made it clearer. @wagamamaz I would like to involve you in the test of the new API. Would you be free for some discussions and maybe some tests ?

TL 2.0 is still under heavy discussion/development, and i would love to have someone like you to have an honest opinion. I can show you the differences and the new TL API and you'll be free to try it ;)

I'm not sure about this. This would mean the LDAP server isn't a single source of truth. It seems like it would hurt the security of the Django site.

A user could be intentionally removed from the LDAP directory, but would still be allowed to login to the Django site. If this user were removed for negative reasons, they may take advantage of still being allowed to login to do harm.

A user could have their password compromised. Maybe they changed it in the LDAP directory, but it would remain the old password in Django. If a malicious user had obtained the compromised password, they would be able to use it to login to the Django site.

Regarding your grammar, when writing for LALR it's better to keep things as simple as possible. It's also better to So for example:

    args: expr expr* kwarg* | kwarg+

Can be rewritten as:

    args: expr+  kwarg* | kwarg+

Or:

One thing probably worth mentioning discussing here for want of a better forum: the next version of this PR I push up of this will likely deprecate ambiguity='explicit'.

The easiest way to think about why this is, is to understand that for Earley today lark does:
Parse->Tree (ambiguous)->Tree (unambiguous)

The problem is that Trees do not fundamentally or efficiently support all kinds of ambiguity that Earley can generate. You see this in some of the deep tree copies that were required in the currently Earley implementation. Most academic work on Earley moved to Forests (SPPF); which can support the ambiguity correctly and efficiently and is what I've implemented here.

The version of this PR you see here does this in order to keep ambiguity = 'explicit':
Parse->Forest (ambiguous)->Tree (ambiguous)->Tree (unambiguous)

But this does show up some of the disparities. I've currently avoided doing deep copies and complex conversions for Forest->Tree by not supporting some kinds of ambiguity which is fine since I have control of my grammars and can restructure them.

The new Earley which I will PR when I have time would do:
Parse->Forest (ambiguous)->Tree (unambiguous)

This would mean the old ambiguity='explicit', would instead be replicated by getting the handle to the Forest, instead of the tree and operations to examine the ambiguity would operate there. The advantages of the is that it would be significantly more efficient (CPU/memory) than either this PR /or/ the current version of Earley in use in lark.

I don't believe this is fundamentally problematic; and over time probably just requires adding some niceties to the Forest implementation to make it 'walk and talk' like a Tree so from a user perspective it should be as simple to work with; and documentation for working with the Forest. To me; ambiguity='explicit' seems to mostly be a development/testing tool anyway, since what you almost always want in practise is an unambiguous tree. But I thought it worth mentioning/discussing since it does make kind of an ethos change and removing ambiguity='explicit' might be a breaking change for some folks.

As one who really wants to use this rpc library, I would not want the apparent interface to expose any inability to just call functions via the proxy. Forcing the users to use args=, kwargs= is pretty rough. 

I like the idea of setting these values on a class if you can allow users to pass their own class to use for method wrapping. Its unlikely a user would want these values to change for specific functions.

[Travis](https://github.com/traviscline/gevent-zeromq) is the only consumer of the Cython API that I know of, so it's not a *huge* deal, but it should be considered carefully, as it is an actively used interface.

The principal Cython use cases I can think of:

1. want to subclass the whole Socket, keeping optimized access to send/recv, etc.
2. want to write their own Cython ZeroMQ code, and only use our simplest wrapping of libzmq to avoid duplicate code.

I imagine 1. would be more common, and if doing something like this breaks it, we should rethink the idea.
